### Post by tseliot on 2006-01-08
[QUOTE=r4ik]Tseliot,
sudo sh NVIDIA-Linux-x86_64-1.0-8178-pkg2.run --uninstall
"unknown"
I also have to set x keyboard (Gnome keyboard doesnt work)
Thinking to do a re-install here and have a fresh start.
Option or do you have any other ideas ?
Actualy i had the 7667 (its still om my desktop) tried to remove it also
"unknown"
Pomdidomdidom...:)
Funny thing is i can set resolutions go full-screen not as good a 128mb should be but i can.
Tried to set nv to nvidia but it crashes.[/QUOTE]
Sorry, my bad.
Type:
cd path_to_the_installer (i.e. where you put the nvidia installer)
sudo sh name_of_your_nvidia_installer.run --uninstall

If the command fail don't you worry and go on with the other steps I told you to follow.

---

### Post by r4ik on 2006-01-08
[QUOTE=tseliot]Sorry, my bad.
Type:
cd path_to_the_installer (i.e. where you put the nvidia installer)
sudo sh name_of_your_nvidia_installer.run --uninstall

If the command fail don't you worry and go on with the other steps I told you to follow.[/QUOTE]

Tseliot,
The commands kept bashing me around (must be my unusual config i think :) )
So this what worked for me.
I re-installed nvidia-glx using Synaptic and did a
"sudo nvidia-glx-config enable" in a Terminal rebooted and the Nvidia logo
popped up.
I have all options enabled so thank you very much for showing me the way
and for your time and effort !
I have to clean up the mess i made a little but fot the moment i happy as can be.:)
Thanks again.

---

### Post by Zerocool10482 on 2006-01-08
Hi, I have a big problem. My screen starts up at 640 x 480. How do I fix this. I have the drivers for my Nvidia FX 5200. It was fine last time but now I can't get it back to normal. I went to Screen Resolution and it's stuck at that setting. Can you give me an easy way to fix this. Besides reinstalling the drivers.

### Post by tseliot on 2006-01-08
[QUOTE=Zerocool10482]Hi, I have a big problem. My screen starts up at 640 x 480. How do I fix this. I have the drivers for my Nvidia FX 5200. It was fine last time but now I can't get it back to normal. I went to Screen Resolution and it's stuck at that setting. Can you give me an easy way to fix this. Besides reinstalling the drivers.[/QUOTE]
That's easy, follow this guide: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=83973&highlight=resolution]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=83973&highlight=resolution")

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-01-08
[QUOTE=r4ik]Tseliot,
The commands kept bashing me around (must be my unusual config i think :) )
So this what worked for me.
I re-installed nvidia-glx using Synaptic and did a
"sudo nvidia-glx-config enable" in a Terminal rebooted and the Nvidia logo
popped up.
I have all options enabled so thank you very much for showing me the way
and for your time and effort !
I have to clean up the mess i made a little but fot the moment i happy as can be.:)
Thanks again.[/QUOTE]
I'm happy it worked in the end :)

---

i attached too, my xorg.conf file and other log file related the xorg. i have tried many times, and i followed method # 2 and when i tried removing nvidia-kernel-common some dependencies related to the kernel were going to be removed to. i tried to install nvidia driver without those dependencies and it didn't work neither, even nvidia driver marked as some missing files but it was installed, and it didn't worked.

i hope this could help you to help me!! thanks tseloit:p :p

i attached too, my xorg.conf file and other log file related the xorg. i have tried many times, and i followed method # 2 and when i tried removing nvidia-kernel-common some dependencies related to the kernel were going to be removed to. i tried to install nvidia driver without those dependencies and it didn't work neither, even nvidia driver marked as some missing files but it was installed, and it didn't worked.

i hope this could help you to help me!! thanks tseloit:p :p 

sincerly nrayever[/QUOTE]
Which version of the drivers did you try when you tried method 2?

If you haven't done it yet you can install the nvidia drivers 8178 (get it from the nvidia website).

Follow these steps:

```
sudo apt-get --purge remove nvidia-glx
```

Press CTRL+ALT+F1 (you will see only the command line)

```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop
OR
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm stop (if you use KDM)
```

Uninstall the driver you installed before with method 2:
```
sudo sh name_of_the_nvidia_installer_you_used_before.run --uninstall
```

```
sudo apt-get install linux-image-686 linux-image-686
```

NOTE: replace 686 with your architecture e.g. 386, k7,etc. (which you can see by typing "uname -r")

```
sudo reboot
```

After Ubuntu boots, type:

```
sudo apt-get --purge remove linux-restricted-modules-`uname -r`
```

Restart your computer again (just to be absolutely sure)

Then follow Method 2 again (follow EVERY step)

Then tell me if it works.

### Post by tseliot on 2006-01-10
[QUOTE=Nameeater]I have a similar error as nrayever (probably the same) X crashes at startup with those errors, I let X crash and then logged in and tried starting up with startx and got a mismatch error (why couldn't that first log have been as helpful?!), something along the lines of it trying to use the latest driver while 7667 is installed.

I can't recall if I installed 7667, but I don't have the package any more.
Do I need to download that package and follow what you posted above or is there another way of removing it?

I also have the latest driver installed as well.

Any help appreciated! :)[/QUOTE]
I suppose your problem is easier than nrayever's (because I know what it is):

Follow these steps:

```
sudo apt-get --purge remove nvidia-glx
```

Press CTRL+ALT+F1 (you will see only the command line)

```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop
OR
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm stop (if you use KDM)
```

Uninstall the driver you installed before with method 2:
```
sudo sh name_of_the_nvidia_installer_you_used_before.run --uninstall
```

```
sudo apt-get install linux-image-686 linux-image-686
```

NOTE: replace 686 with your architecture e.g. 386, k7,etc. (which you can see by typing "uname -r")

```
sudo reboot
```

After Ubuntu boots, type:

```
sudo apt-get --purge remove linux-restricted-modules-`uname -r`
```

Restart your computer again (just to be absolutely sure)

Then follow Method 2 again (follow EVERY step)

Then tell me if it works.

### Post by tseliot on 2006-01-10
[QUOTE=nrayever]tseliot:

i used the 8178 drivers, but today i realized that might be a mobo problem, because i was trying to set a pair of 1gb kingston ddr, and i couldn't! mobo always make beeps as a memory error, but when i tried with one gb, nvidia driver worked fine.

so i need to check this problems. thanks a lot!!

sincerly nrayever[/QUOTE]
1) When does the motherboard makes beeps? Before starting Ubuntu?

2) try to swap the RAM modules (i.e. try with only one of them per time) so as to see if one of them is damaged.

3) make sure you put them correctly (I know it's a naive answer..)

4) Do you have the same problem in Windows (if you use it)?

### Post by CarinArr on 2006-01-11
[QUOTE=jeffreyvergara.NET]hi, sorry if this question was asked before, but I dont have much time to read other post.

is it possible to use Method 2 installation after using Method 1? or do i still need to do additional steps?

Thanks[/QUOTE]

If you've already got the nvidia drivers installed through the package manager you have to make sure you completely remove them before you install the ones from the nvidia website.. (this includes removing the linux-restricted-modules packages). How you do this has been described earlier in this thread. If you don't uninstall them properly you'll run into problems after you've isntalled the new drivers.

startx -- -verbose 5 -logverbose 5

and post the output (which you can find under /var/log/Xorg.0.log )

### Post by borisattva on 2006-01-12
as of right now this isnt a very good guide as it does not make any provisions for case of someone with gcc 4.0 already installed (and not knowing it)

i followed the instructions to the letter and got gcc version mismatch. nvidia requires  3.4 and when attempting to do it with 4.0 it fails.

if the cc=gcc-3.4 line was supposed to make that the primary compiler it was not succesfull. several trials into the attempt i decided to remove the gcc4.0 and reinstalled gcc3.4 thinking it will make it default. it did not, now the installer cannot find the cc at all. also removing gcc removed dependencies which i thought would be automatically included when i would reinstall it. i just tried it and it did not envoke any related applications for installation. time will tell what i'll be missing from this.

i do not mean any offence with my critisism but HOW TO's should be as versatile as possible in a walkthrough and not anticipating a current (and incompatible as it turned out) component which is the most likely to be present in fresh installers/ newbies like me is a big let down. 

perhaps updating and incorporating the neuances that came from the follow up posts into the threads and cleaning the posts out (60+ pages? i got a headache after reading about 30 of them and gave up) is in order.

otherwise i trully apprecaite the effort you gave to make this, hopefully it can be updated soon.

---

### Post by Worm_in_a_Box on 2006-01-12
Hey, I had the same problem some minutes ago, I solved it this way:
After screwing gcc stuff:

sudo apt-get install gcc 
sudo apt-get install gcc-3.4 

Loged in the terminal, turned x down , then:
su
CC=gcc-3.4
export CC
exit
CC=gcc-3.4
export CC
sh thenameofyourdiver.bl&#225;bl&#225;lb&#225;

(Make sure to use the capitals!)
Hope that this helps you like it helped me. By the way, the driver seens to be installed, but it is running really slow in 3d stuff, when I go to glxgear I get like 300fps, and it never was lower than 800. Anyone know how to fix it?

---

export CC

### Post by tseliot on 2006-01-13
[QUOTE=borisattva]sudo NVIDIA_drive.run -- uninstall didnt get recognized as a command,
so i tried it with sh after sudo, which worked.

i did a reboot, set gcc-3.4 and reinstalled, process OK. gnome loads ok, with screen properly aligned indicating succesfull driver install. but
another reboot - back to the original error with 7667 to 8178 (current) mismatch.

its so bizare that it can actually work after installation, and then error out after a rebooted.[/QUOTE]
..Weird

Try this:
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```
OR
```
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm stop (if you use kDM)
```

```
startx -- -verbose 5 -logverbose 5
```

and post the output (which you can find under /var/log/Xorg.0.log )

BTW if you want to access the GNOME or KDE again you can use this quick fix:
```
sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf
```
Set the driver back to "nv" instead of "nvidia"
CTRL+O to save
CTRL+X to exit

EDIT:
and of course you have to Type:
```
startx
```

### Post by borisattva on 2006-01-13
when i began reading your last responce in the morning, i began to wonder if i actually stopped gdm before i tried the driver install last time. i checked the command history and sure enough i did NOT. (it was 5 am here at that time, i was .. fried)

so i --remove'd restricted modules again (this time there was only 1)
and uninstalled the driver.
i rebooted and reattempted with GDM disabled before everything else, driver loaded fine, i rebooted again and voila, it got me straight into gnome. 

problem solved.

it looks like setting the gcc in both su and regular,
and removing the restricted modules prior to upgrade were the missing steps for my particular set up. hopefully these findings will benefit others in the future as well.

thanks for all your help tseliot.
youre dedication to this forum is trully respectable.

### Post by SolidAndShade on 2006-01-14
Thanks for the guide, tseliot. I've used it three times to enable Geforce 6150 support  (multiple installs on same computer, long story) but this latest time it doesn't work. The nvidia install program tells me that it can't find the kernel source, despite the fact that I installed the kernel-source package and I can see the source bz2 file right there in /usr/src. If I try to tell the installer the location of the source by using --kernel-source-location or whatever the option it, it fails as well. I've tried unpacking the source bz2 file and pointing the installer to the include/linux directory inside and doing a bunch of other things, but it still refuses to recognize the source as beloning to the current kernel. I'm running the current version of the amdk7-smp kernel with the corresponding headers, gcc 3.4 and everything else that allowed me to successfully install the drivers the last three times. Do you have any idea what could be going wrong? Thanks.

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-01-14
[QUOTE=SolidAndShade]Thanks for the guide, tseliot. I've used it three times to enable Geforce 6150 support  (multiple installs on same computer, long story) but this latest time it doesn't work. The nvidia install program tells me that it can't find the kernel source, despite the fact that I installed the kernel-source package and I can see the source bz2 file right there in /usr/src. If I try to tell the installer the location of the source by using --kernel-source-location or whatever the option it, it fails as well. I've tried unpacking the source bz2 file and pointing the installer to the include/linux directory inside and doing a bunch of other things, but it still refuses to recognize the source as beloning to the current kernel. I'm running the current version of the amdk7-smp kernel with the corresponding headers, gcc 3.4 and everything else that allowed me to successfully install the drivers the last three times. Do you have any idea what could be going wrong? Thanks.[/QUOTE]
Ok, try this:
```
cd /usr/src[CODE]
[CODE]sudo tar -xjvf name_of_the_tarred_source
```
Then a new folder will be created
Do a symbolic link (named "linux") to that folder:
```
sudo ln -s name_of_the_new_folder linux
```
Then when you use the installer use this option:
```
--kernel-source-location=/usr/src/linux
```

### Post by tseliot on 2006-01-15
[QUOTE=Rasymas]What's an output? anyway, here's my xorg.conf[/CODE]
It means what you get from a command (i.e. the result)

Have a look at this section of your file:
[QUOTE=Rasymas]
Section "Device"
    Identifier     "NVIDIA Corporation NV11 [GeForce2 MX/MX 400]"
    Driver         "nv"
EndSection[/QUOTE]

And make it look like this:

```
Section "Device"
    Identifier     "NVIDIA Corporation NV11 [GeForce2 MX/MX 400]"
    Driver         "nvidia"
EndSection
```

CTRL+O to save the file (yes, overwrite it)
CTRL+X to exit

Then log out and press CTRL+ALT+Backspace.

And it should work

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-01-15
[QUOTE=Rasymas]Copy from PM:

Method1:

1. "sudo apt-get install nvidia-glx nvidia-settings linux-restricted-modules-386"

arnoldas@ubuntu:~$ sudo apt-get install nvidia-glx nvidia-settings linux-restricted-modules-386
Reading package lists... Done
Building dependency tree... Done
nvidia-glx is already the newest version.
nvidia-settings is already the newest version.
linux-restricted-modules-386 is already the newest version.
0 upgraded, 0 newly installed, 0 to remove and 0 not upgraded.

I do have all repositories enabled, but when I want to open and check that source.list it doesn't open.

2. "arnoldas@ubuntu:~$ sudo apt-get install nvidia-glx-legacy"

nvidia-settings-linux-restricted-modules-386-nvidia-legacy
Reading package lists... Done
Building dependency tree... Done
nvidia-settings is already the newest version.
E: Couldn't find package linux-restricted-modules-386-nvidia-legacy[/QUOTE]
Hey, wait a second you have to install ONLY the "nvidia-glx-legacy" and the restricted modules for nvidia legacy for your card.
When I write to do one thing "OR" another I don't mean that you have to do both things.

Make sure ALL your repositories are enabled.
And PLEASE read the guide carefully.

BTW these are the right commands (copy and paste them):
```
sudo apt-get --purge remove nvidia-glx
```

then

```
sudo apt-get install nvidia-glx-legacy nvidia-settings linux-restricted-modules-`uname -r`-nvidia-legacy
```

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-01-15
[QUOTE=Rasymas]First tip doesn't work, same blue window. 
Second tip: arnoldas@ubuntu:~$ sudo apt-get install nvidia-glx-legacy nvidia-settings linux-restricted-modules-386-nvidia-legacy
Reading package lists... Done
Building dependency tree... Done
nvidia-glx-legacy is already the newest version.
nvidia-settings is already the newest version.
E: Couldn't find package linux-restricted-modules-386-nvidia-legacy[/QUOTE]
This command:
sudo apt-get install nvidia-glx-legacy nvidia-settings linux-restricted-modules-386-nvidia-legacy

is not the same as the one I suggested i.e. the following:
sudo apt-get install nvidia-glx-legacy nvidia-settings linux-restricted-modules-`uname -r`-nvidia-legacy

This means that the package you need is NOT "linux-restricted-modules-386-nvidia-legacy" (which you probably invented) but "linux-restricted-modules-`uname -r`-nvidia-legacy"

When I tell you to "copy and paste" the command, please do it.

After you have installed the right package.

sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf

And set the driver to nvidia as in the example:
```
Section "Device"
    Identifier     "NVIDIA Corporation NV11 [GeForce2 MX/MX 400]"
    Driver         "[COLOR="Red"]nvidia[/COLOR]"
EndSection
```

CTRL+O to save the file (yes, overwrite it)
CTRL+X to exit

Then log out and press CTRL+ALT+Backspace.

### Post by tseliot on 2006-01-16
[QUOTE=Carrots171]Hi, and thanks for your guide. I've sucessfully installed the latest Nvidia drivers.

Unfortuanately, it seems to be causing some bugs with my graphics card. (I've heard that the newer drivers can be buggy on certain cards). Could someone tell me how to uninstall the drivers so I can just get the older ones in the repository using apt? This is probably simple but since I'm such a n00b I don't know how to do it![/QUOTE]
If you want to get back to your old drivers:

CTRL+ALT+F1

sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (OR "kdm stop" if you use KDE)

cd /home/your_username/path_to_the_installer_you_used/

sudo sh name_of_the_nvidia_installer --uninstall

sudo apt-get install nvidia-glx nvidia-settings linux-restricted-modules-`uname -r`

**OR** (If you need Legacy drivers, i.e. your graphic card is in the list at the end of the page or you need driver 7174)

```
sudo apt-get install nvidia-glx-legacy nvidia-settings linux-restricted-modules-`uname -r`-nvidia-legacy
``` 

then start the xserver again:

sudo /etc/init.d/gdm start (OR "kdm start" if you use KDE)

### Post by tseliot on 2006-01-16
[QUOTE=william_nbg]My questions:  I have the geforce 6600 -  256mb memory, running a 3.2 AMD with the K7 kernel - would there be a big performance jump if I upgraded to the 8178 driver using method 2??  Is it worth the risk??[/QUOTE]
I don't use my graphic card to play games in  Linux therefore I don't know if the improvement will be visible. However if you plan to use the composite extension (you know, transparencies, and other thing to make your desktop more eyecandy etc.) the new driver will make it much more stable. I'm currently using the latest version of the drivers with no problems whatsoever. 
The decision is down to you.

[QUOTE=william_nbg]Second question: Would points 7,8,9, in method 2 instructions be enough to take out the 7667 driver completely.[/QUOTE]

Yes, they should do it. I updated the guide and added those steps in order to be sure to get rid of the older driver. 

Moreover I have added point 5. It will make you install a new kernel which won't have the modules for the old driver.

# /etc/X11/xorg.conf (xorg X Window System server configuration file)

Cheers.[/QUOTE]
Thanks for appreciating my work.

First off I have 2 questions for you:
1) Do you see only a blank screen? No writings at all?
2) Do you use an LCD display?
If you do, do you use an Analog o Digital (DVI) cable?

Anyhow I need to reproduce the error in order to detect the problem.
Therefore, please follow these steps:

Press CTRL+ALT+F1 (so as to get out of the xserver, i.e. no graphical interface)

Then type:
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```
OR
```
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm stop (if you use kDM)
```

```
startx -- -verbose 5 -logverbose 5
```

and post the output (which you can find under /var/log/Xorg.0.log )

### Post by firehead on 2006-01-16
@Rasymas
> Gree-tings Mas-ter Tse-liot,

Can-you-tell-me-why-my-Sources.list-does-not-open?

sudo gedit /etc/apt/sources.list

well i know i'm not tseliot, but i will try to help you anyway... 
what exactly do you mean by saying your sources.list won't open? do you get any kind of error message or do you just get a blank page in gedit? maybe you accidentally removed/cleaned your sources.list...

if you get an error message, it would be very helpful if you posted it here, so someone could give you the solution to your very problem.

otherwise, if you just get a blank page, you could simply copy-paste a sources.list that's posted in this forum e.g. here (if you use breezy): [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=92672](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=92672)
then type
```
sudo apt-get update
```
afterwoods you can use ```
sudo apt-get install package-name
``` as usual to install your desired program...

i hope this helps a little....

### Post by nrayever on 2006-01-16
[QUOTE=tseliot]Use the gigabyte k8ns with all your RAM modules and you enter the BIOS setup.
Is all your RAM detected there?

BTW if your motherboard is still under warranty you can have it fixed.[/QUOTE]

tseliot:

thanks for the advice, but i had tried once to do that with another mobo and the other mobo finished worst, it was not working anymore. so i prefer to sold this one, the k8ns. right now my amd64 processor and mobo are being used in a little local tv news here at my city. and now i have a p4 @ 2.4 ghz with 2gb running at dual channel.

and finally after a lot of figthing i make my video card work with the latest nvidia drivers 8178. here is a possible tip for your guide. this was using help of my friend sonny, another ubuntu user, that right now is a bit away from ubuntu and giving a shot with mandriva. he will return with ubuntu when dapper is ready. as a quote he thinks that mandriva is faraway from ubuntu.

after following all the step in method #2, and with X.org running the nvidia driver. you need to remove the nvidia-kernel-common, so go to synaptic and completelly remove it, when if other dependencies are removed too. so with this you fix the problem that the X.org has selecting between driver 7667 and the new driver. then after a reboot, your nvidia driver will be the latest, no more confusions problems for the X.org!!:D :D 

you may think that removing the other dependencies might make the system unstable or something, but until now my system works pretty nice!!

this is my contrubution to your guide. i hope this will help others to solve their problems.

sincerly nrayever

---

### Post by firehead on 2006-01-16
> Well, thanks in advance

Here's what happens. I type "sudo gedit... sources.list" and I wait. wait wait wait waaaaaa-it. Nothing pops up, no errors. Nothing happens 
hmmm...sounds to me like there's something wrong with gedit... have you ever tried nano??
```
sudo nano /etc/apt/sources.list
```
it's a little more old-fashioned text-editor,but usually works...
if it's really a gedit issue i'm not to sure wether i could help you further (since a) i use KDE and b) i'm quite a noob myself ;) ), but i'm sure someone will...
let us know wether nano worked or not...

---

### Post by nszabolcs on 2006-01-16
[QUOTE=Rasymas]Well, thanks in advance ;)

Here's what happens. I type "sudo gedit... sources.list" and I wait. wait wait wait waaaaaa-it. Nothing pops up, no errors. Nothing happens :)[/QUOTE]

does gedit work without sudo?
when i first installed breezy i skipped a few installation step and i ended up with a broken sudo under gnome. (ie. any gnome application startup was more than 5 min with sudo. eg. try 'sudo nautilus')

i couldn't fix the problem so i quickly reinstalled the whole thing

hope that helps

### Post by tseliot on 2006-01-17
[QUOTE=nrayever]after following all the step in method #2, and with X.org running the nvidia driver. you need to remove the nvidia-kernel-common, so go to synaptic and completelly remove it, when if other dependencies are removed too. so with this you fix the problem that the X.org has selecting between driver 7667 and the new driver. then after a reboot, your nvidia driver will be the latest, no more confusions problems for the X.org!!:D :D 

you may think that removing the other dependencies might make the system unstable or something, but until now my system works pretty nice!!

this is my contrubution to your guide. i hope this will help others to solve their problems.

sincerly nrayever[/QUOTE]

### Post by tseliot on 2006-01-17
[QUOTE=rikoshay]Here's my nvidia installer log...[/QUOTE]
I can't see any error in that process.

type (copy and paste):
cd /lib/modules/`uname -r`/kernel/drivers/video/
ls

and post the output.

It's pretty crazy when you have to configure the internet each time you load up pc. Anyway, nszabolcs, nautilus or whatever that is, didn't work, but thanks anyway ;)

Sorry for a bit of OFF TOPIC :P

### Post by rikoshay on 2006-01-17
TS,

Thanks for all your help. I've actually managed to solve the problem by switching from the DVI to the analogue cable and reinstalling the 8178 drivers using method 2. In order to get a 1280x1024 screen resolution I also had to remove the previous changes I'd made using your screen resolution how-to.

Here's my xorg for interest:

```
# nvidia-xconfig: X configuration file generated by nvidia-xconfig
# nvidia-xconfig:  version 1.0  (buildmeister@builder3)  Wed Dec 14 16:39:22 PST 2005

    InputDevice    "Generic Keyboard"

EndSection

Section "Files"

        # paths to defoma fonts
    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/misc"

    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/100dpi/:unscaled"

    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/75dpi"
    FontPath        "/var/lib/defoma/x-ttcidfont-conf.d/dirs/TrueType"
    FontPath        "/var/lib/defoma/x-ttcidfont-conf.d/dirs/CID"
EndSection

Section "Module"

    #Load	   "GLcore"
    Load           "bitmap"
    Load           "dbe"
    Load           "ddc"
    #Load	   "dri"
    Load           "extmod"
    Load           "freetype"
    Load           "glx"
    Load           "int10"
    Load           "record"
    Load           "type1"
    Load           "vbe"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier     "Generic Keyboard"
    Driver         "kbd"
    Option         "CoreKeyboard"
    Option         "XkbRules" "xorg"
    Option         "XkbModel" "pc105"
    Option         "XkbLayout" "gb"
    Option         "XkbVariant" "gb"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier     "Configured Mouse"
    Driver         "mouse"
    Option         "CorePointer"
    Option         "Device" "/dev/input/mice"
    Option         "Protocol" "ImPS/2"
    Option         "Emulate3Buttons" "true"
    Option         "ZAxisMapping" "4 5"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"

# V-freq: 60.00 Hz  // h-freq: 63.73 KHz
    Identifier     "F-419"
    HorizSync       24.0 - 80.0
    VertRefresh     50.0 - 75.0
EndSection

Section "Device"
    Identifier     "NVIDIA Corporation NV34 [GeForce FX 5200]"
    Driver         "nvidia"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
    Identifier     "Default Screen"
    Device         "NVIDIA Corporation NV34 [GeForce FX 5200]"
    Monitor        "F-419"
    DefaultDepth    24
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       1
        Modes      "1280x1024" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       4
        Modes      "1280x1024" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       8
        Modes      "1280x1024" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       15
        Modes      "1280x1024" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       16
        Modes      "1280x1024" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       24
        Modes      "1280x1024" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
EndSection


```

Tomorrow I'm going to have a go getting the TV-out to work with your next how-to.

Cheers and keep up the excellent work!

:)

### Post by n0fx on 2006-01-17
I was wondering if anyone can help me with my weird problem.  I installed Breezy and it detected my card as Nvidia unknown.  I used automatix to install the NVIDIA drivers and afterwards, the display on the desktop would work for a while then the screen would messup, the screen would not refresh properly or at all.  So, I found this thread and decided to upgrade the latest drivers from Nvidia, which was 1.0-8178 (32-bit).

I managed to follow the guide (method #2), except I could not get the remove the restricted-modules because I was using a custom compiled kernel (2.6.14cK1, patched).  The driver installed fine and after I rebooted, I couldn't log into the desktop.  It would freeze at the Ubuntu splash screen and the mouse wouldn't move at all, so I added these lines to my /etc/X11/xorg.conf file:

BusID "PCI:1:0:0"
Option "NvAGP" "0"
Option "RenderAccel" "Off"
Option "IgnoreDisplayDevices" "DFP,TV"
Option "NoRenderExtension" "Off"
Option "Accel" "Off"
Option "AllowGLXWithComposite" “Off”

It managed to let me login into the desktop but when I run glxgears to test the FPS, I tried to move the window across the desktop and it caused the whole computer to freeze.  I was wondering how would I fix this?  The video card that I'm using is the Asustek N6200TC (Geforce 6200 Turbo Charged) PCI-X.

### Post by tseliot on 2006-01-18
[QUOTE=n0fx]I was wondering if anyone can help me with my weird problem.  I installed Breezy and it detected my card as Nvidia unknown.  I used automatix to install the NVIDIA drivers and afterwards, the display on the desktop would work for a while then the screen would messup, the screen would not refresh properly or at all.  So, I found this thread and decided to upgrade the latest drivers from Nvidia, which was 1.0-8178 (32-bit).

I managed to follow the guide (method #2), except I could not get the remove the restricted-modules because I was using a custom compiled kernel (2.6.14cK1, patched).  The driver installed fine and after I rebooted, I couldn't log into the desktop.  It would freeze at the Ubuntu splash screen and the mouse wouldn't move at all, so I added these lines to my /etc/X11/xorg.conf file:

BusID "PCI:1:0:0"
Option "NvAGP" "0"
Option "RenderAccel" "Off"
Option "IgnoreDisplayDevices" "DFP,TV"
Option "NoRenderExtension" "Off"
Option "Accel" "Off"
Option "AllowGLXWithComposite" &#8220;Off&#8221;

It managed to let me login into the desktop but when I run glxgears to test the FPS, I tried to move the window across the desktop and it caused the whole computer to freeze.  I was wondering how would I fix this?  The video card that I'm using is the Asustek N6200TC (Geforce 6200 Turbo Charged) PCI-X.[/QUOTE]
My father uses a Geforce 6200 Turbo Charge PCI-E (I don't know the brand though).

I need you to reproduce the error.
Therefore
Press CTRL+ALT+F1

sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf
Comment these options out (i.e. put a "#" at the beginning of each of those lines) i.e. make them look as in the following example:

#Option "NvAGP" "0"
#Option "RenderAccel" "Off"
#Option "IgnoreDisplayDevices" "DFP,TV"
#Option "NoRenderExtension" "Off"
#Option "Accel" "Off"
#Option "AllowGLXWithComposite" &#8220;Off&#8221;

Then
CTRL+O
CTRL+X

Then:
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```
OR
```
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm stop (if you use kDM)
```

```
startx -- -verbose 5 -logverbose 5
```

and post the output (which you can find under /var/log/Xorg.0.log )

BTW if you want to access the GNOME or KDE again you can use this quick fix:
```
sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf
```
Set the driver back to "nv" instead of "nvidia"
CTRL+O to save
CTRL+X to exit

EDIT:
and of course you have to Type:
```
startx
```

If it freezes you can restart in Recovery mode (select it from the Grub menu i.e. the menu in which you can choose the kernel to boot in).

type this:
sudo cp /var/log/Xorg.0.log /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log

Of course you have to replace "your_username" with your username

Then

open your Xorg.conf and put the options back (uncomment them i.e. remove the "#")

Then restart your computer, i.e. type:
reboot

You will find an "Xorg.0.log" in your home folder. Please post it.



Then you will find an Xorg.0.log in your home folder. Please post its content

And put the options back.

[COLOR="Red"]EDIT: another IMPORTANT thing: access to your BIOS and select your video card: you won't be able to choose the model but you will be able to choose between AGP, PCI and PCI-E (maybe AGP won't show up at all).
My father experienced several lockups because the port of the video card was set as PCI (but the card is PCI-E)[/COLOR]

---

### Post by jtpratt on 2006-01-19
I have tried every method in your guide to install my drivers for 3 days and nights with no luck.  I have an old Gateway with on mobo integrated Intel graphics controller, and I bought a GeForce FX 5200 PCI I want to install which came with DVI output you have to use a standard monitor adapter on to work.  I have kernal 2.6.12-10-386 and gcc version 3.45 on ubuntu 3.4.4-6.

I think maybe my problem is that the guide has no specifics as to when or where to do what.  For example, I have working onboard video now.  I have to print out the guide, to what I can from Gnome, shut down, install the video card, boot to recovery mode to finish, then reboot or startx to test what I've done.  The guide assumes your card is in and working, and you just need to enable nvidia drivers or 3d accelleration.

Let's go through the issues:
Method 1 won't work: I don't understand how it would work because it doesn't have you update your xorg.conf file at all.  I guess maybe sudo nvidia-glx-config enable is supposed to do it, but it doesn't.  I have tried to do the entire thing as root in recovery mode and it doesn't update xorg.conf at all.

Method 2 won't work:  I've set root password, purged old drivers as listed, but this section appears to be not working:
su
CC=gcc-3.4
export CC
exit
CC=gcc-3.4
export CC

I get no error when I type these, but every single time I run the nvidia installer it says that it can't find gcc 3.4 and the install fails.  Every time.

Method 3 won't work:  it bombs every time when trying to startx.

I decided to try the advice in the response directly after your guide for FX 5200 since it was specific for that card.  I reinstalled the drivers using apt-get, did the xorg.conf backup, and nvidia-glx-config enable (this is wrong in that post it has a dash between config and enable that needs to be removed).  I think the problem here is that the nvidia-glx-config enable still wasn't updating my xorg.conf file.

I opened xorg.conf as root and saw my old intel driver.  I changed the identifier to "NVIDIA Corporation NV34 [GeForce FX 5200]".  The BusID was set to PCI:0:2:0.  I did lspci and found that was the ID for the old Intel video controller.  It said 0000:01:09:0 for my nvidia card, so I changed the PCI busid to PCI:1:9:0.  I saw no mention of setting your busid in your guide but found this in other threads.  I changed the driver to "nvidia", and added these lines:
Option "RenderAccel" "On"
Option "IgnoreDisplayDevices" "DFP,TV"
Option "NoRenderExtension "On"
Option "Accel" "On"
Option "AllowGLXWithComposite" "Off"

after saving xorg.conf when I did startx - I got a half black and half white screen and then lockup.  I edited xorg.conf again and commented out those lines above, and got an all black screen and lockup again.

I think I have the right busid now and it seems like it 'wants' to startx, but something is still not right.  I've provided all the info I could - let me know if you need anymore and any help is appreciated.  I have taken out and reinstalled this card a dozen times now.

### Post by tseliot on 2006-01-19
[QUOTE=jtpratt]...The guide assumes your card is in and working, and you just need to enable nvidia drivers or 3d accelleration.[/QUOTE]
That's right, the installation of a new card is not covered by the guide yet.

Try this: 
boot in Recovery mode
```
dpkg-reconfigure xserver-org
```
select you videocard and the "nv" (driver for now). Whenever you find a question for which you don't know the answer just press ENTER.
Another thing: if you have to enable the right resolution you have to do it with the spacebar (then you can press ENTER).

Then reboot:
```
reboot
```

Access your BIOS and disable your Integrated graphic card. Perhaps you can choose between PCI and AGP (well, I don't know your BIOS).

Then boot Ubuntu.

If you manage to use the graphic card then you can try to install the nvidia drivers.

About the DVI: there are some users who have problems with the drivers. In that case a VGA cable (or an adapter) is the solution.


[QUOTE=jtpratt]Let's go through the issues:
Method 1 won't work: I don't understand how it would work because it doesn't have you update your xorg.conf file at all.  I guess maybe sudo nvidia-glx-config enable is supposed to do it, but it doesn't.  I have tried to do the entire thing as root in recovery mode and it doesn't update xorg.conf at all.[/QUOTE]
You're not the 1st user who has this problem. It's the official method.
In that case you have to type:
sudo gedit /etc/X11/xorg.conf
or
sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf

and set the driver from "nv" to "nvidia"

[QUOTE=jtpratt]Method 2 won't work:  I've set root password, purged old drivers as listed, but this section appears to be not working:
su
CC=gcc-3.4
export CC
exit
CC=gcc-3.4
export CC

I get no error when I type these, but every single time I run the nvidia installer it says that it can't find gcc 3.4 and the install fails.  Every time.[/QUOTE]
Have a look at point 3 of the PROBLEMS SECTION.

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-01-19
[QUOTE=rykel]nvidia should see this thread... it is soooo long it just knocked me over! really, installing ANY software on linux should be made much, MUCH easier...      :confused: [/QUOTE]
My guide is long because it includes 3 methods (you only have to use 1 of them) and a problems section. It has to contain as much information as possible in order several problems which can arise.

[QUOTE=rykel]btw, to those who wonder "why" we must use the official nvidia driver: the reason is simply coz we do NOT have the latest version in the ubuntu repositories, and there are no autopackages available to quickly install the latest version.[/QUOTE]
That's Ubuntu developers' decision. Anyhow it's not necessary to use the latest driver. If it works don't touch it.

### Post by tseliot on 2006-01-19
[QUOTE=zubrug]...My card works fine in linspire, would like the same in ubuntu.[/QUOTE]
I think Linspire  uses Xfree86 (which is older than Xorg) but this is not a problem.

About your problem:

First off plug in your Nvidia card

Access your BIOS and disable your Integrated graphic card. Perhaps you can choose between PCI (the integrated card) and AGP.

Then keep pressing ESC as soon as you turn on your computer.
You will get to the GRUB menu. Select "Recovery mode" and press ENTER.
It will boot in the command line (the xserver and the Graphical interface won't be started)

Type:
```
dpkg-reconfigure xserver-org
```

select your Nvidia card and the "nv" (driver for now). Whenever you find a question for which you don't know the answer just press ENTER.
Another thing: if you have to enable the right resolution you have to do it with the spacebar (then you can press ENTER).

Then reboot:
```
reboot
```

And boot Ubuntu.

If you manage to use the Nvidia card then you can try to install the nvidia drivers.

---

Purging configuration files for nvidia-settings ... Errors were encountered while processing: nvidia-glx E: Sub-process /usr/bin/dpkg returned an error code (1)

### Post by tseliot on 2006-01-19
[QUOTE=t0bb3]I kept trying on my own before I read your reply.
I tried to remove the packages in Synaptic (nvidia-glx and nvidia-settings). It seemed to work all right, so I went on with the installation.
How do I know if it worked as it's supposed to?

I'm running Ubuntu64 if that matters...

//Tobbe[/QUOTE]
If you followed all the instructions then the installation went fine.

As far as glxgears is concerned, I think you shouldn't rely upon its results.

Maybe your AGP is disabled

try this:
cat /proc/driver/nvidia/agp/status

and post the output


Another thing you could do to increase the speed of some effects on your desktop you can add the option in red to your xorg.conf (in its Section "Device") :

```
Section "Device"
	Identifier	"NVIDIA Corporation NV34 [GeForce FX 5500]"
	Driver		"nvidia"
	[COLOR="Red"]Option		"RenderAccel" "True"[/COLOR]
	BusID		"PCI:1:0:0"
EndSection
```

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-01-19
[QUOTE=zubrug]TS
I checked the bios, I have no agp slots, choices are PCI or intergrated. I left it at PCI and proceded.
after dpkg-reconfigure xserver-org I got a message " xserver-org not found"[/QUOTE]
I'm terribly sorry, it was a typo. The correct command is:
dpkg-reconfigure xserver-[COLOR="Red"]x[/COLOR]org

### Post by tseliot on 2006-01-20
[QUOTE=adamthompson]I have been trying to get a Biostar Tforce6100-939 motherboard working. Method 2 did not work at all for me with 64-bit Ubuntu. So I installed 32-bit Ubuntu, and Method 2 seemed to work. I was able to run the NVIDIA installer and it told me that it installed the new (8178) drivers just fine. However, GDM still crashes at start-up, just like it did before it installed the new driver. And when I type "/etc/init.d/gdm start", I get "fail". Any ideas would be much appreciated. Thanks.[/QUOTE]
I need you to reproduce the error.
Therefore set the driver to "nvidia" in your xorg.conf
When GDM crashes at start-up Press CTRL+ALT+F1
Then type

Then:
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```

```
startx -- -verbose 5 -logverbose 5
```

You will get the same error.

sudo cp /var/log/Xorg.0.log /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log
(Of course you have to replace "your_username" with your username)

and post the content of that file (which you can find under /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log )


BTW if you want to access the GNOME or KDE again you can use this quick fix:
```
sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf
```
Set the driver back to "nv" instead of "nvidia"
CTRL+O to save
CTRL+X to exit

```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```

Then
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm start (if you use GDM)
```

Section "Files"
	FontPath	"/usr/share/X11/fonts/misc"

EndSection

EndSection

### Post by tseliot on 2006-01-20
[QUOTE=zubrug]Hi Ts (good morning in Grand Bend)...[/QUOTE]
Save the following text (with the exception of the words I'm writing :p ) to a text file e.g. tseliot.txt and put it in your home folder

Install the Nvidia drivers but DO NOT set the driver to "nvidia", therefore answer NO when the installer of Method2 asks you if you want it to set the xorg for you (I think it's the last question it asks after compiling the module).

[COLOR="Red"]DON'T STARTX (i.e. don't do gdm start)[/COLOR] after you install the Nvidia driver (i.e. stay in the command line).

Then type:
sudo cp /etc/X11/xorg.conf /etc/X11/xorg.old
Make sure you are in your home folder:
ls tseliot*
If it returns "tseliot.txt" then it's ok (otherwise get to your home folder)
sudo cp tseliot.txt /etc/X11/xorg.conf
startx

Here is your new xorg.conf:

# /etc/X11/xorg.conf (xorg X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.
#
# Edit this file with caution, and see the /etc/X11/xorg.conf manual page.
# (Type "man /etc/X11/xorg.conf" at the shell prompt.)
#
# This file is automatically updated on xserver-xorg package upgrades *only*
# if it has not been modified since the last upgrade of the xserver-xorg
# package.
#
# If you have edited this file but would like it to be automatically updated
# again, run the following command:
# sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg

Section "Files"
FontPath "/usr/share/X11/fonts/misc"
FontPath "/usr/share/X11/fonts/cyrillic"
FontPath "/usr/share/X11/fonts/100dpi/:unscaled"
FontPath "/usr/share/X11/fonts/75dpi/:unscaled"
FontPath "/usr/share/X11/fonts/Type1"
FontPath "/usr/share/X11/fonts/CID"
FontPath "/usr/share/X11/fonts/100dpi"
FontPath "/usr/share/X11/fonts/75dpi"
# paths to defoma fonts
FontPath "/var/lib/defoma/x-ttcidfont-conf.d/dirs/TrueType"
FontPath "/var/lib/defoma/x-ttcidfont-conf.d/dirs/CID"
EndSection

Section "Module"
Load "GLcore"
Load "i2c"
Load "bitmap"
Load "ddc"
Load "dri"
Load "extmod"
Load "freetype"
Load "glx"
Load "int10"
Load "type1"
Load "vbe"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
Identifier "Generic Keyboard"
Driver "kbd"
Option "CoreKeyboard"
Option "XkbRules" "xorg"
Option "XkbModel" "pc104"
Option "XkbLayout" "us"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
Identifier "Configured Mouse"
Driver "mouse"
Option "CorePointer"
Option "Device" "/dev/input/mice"
Option "Protocol" "ImPS/2"
Option "Emulate3Buttons" "true"
Option "ZAxisMapping" "4 5"
EndSection

Section "Device"
Identifier "Device1"
Driver "nvidia"
BusID "PCI:0:2:0"
EndSection

Section "Device"
Identifier "Device2"
Driver "nvidia"
Option "nologo"
BusID "PCI:2:10:0"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
Identifier "Screen1"
Device "Device1"
Monitor "Monitor1"
DefaultDepth 24
SubSection "Display"
Depth 24
Modes "1280x1024" 
EndSubSection
EndSection

Section "Screen"
Identifier "Screen2"
Device "Device2"
Monitor "Monitor2"
DefaultDepth 24
SubSection "Display"
Depth 24
Modes "1280x1024" 
EndSubSection
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
Identifier "Monitor1"
VertRefresh 43-62
HorizSync 28-69
Option "DPMS" "on"
UseModes "extra"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
Identifier "Monitor2"
VertRefresh 43-62
HorizSync 28-69
Option "DPMS" "on"
UseModes "extra"
EndSection

Section "Modes"
Identifier "extra"
# 1280x800 @ 60.00 Hz (GTF) hsync: 49.68 kHz; pclk: 83.46 MHz
Modeline "1280x800" 83.5 1280 1344 1480 1680 800 801 804 828 -HSync +VSync
# 1280x800 @ 75.00 Hz (GTF) hsync: 62.62 kHz; pclk: 107.21 MHz
ModeLine "1280x800" 107.2 1280 1360 1496 1712 800 801 804 835

Screen "Screen2"
InputDevice "Generic Keyboard"
InputDevice "Configured Mouse"
EndSection

Section "DRI"
Mode 0666
EndSection

### Post by tseliot on 2006-01-20
[QUOTE=zubrug]TS
I followed step two as well as the problem solvers for cc but still ended up with an cc error install failed.
Should I try step one or three?[/QUOTE]
type:
sudo apt-get install gcc-3.4

and do the "Export" thing again. Then try to run the installer again.

If it doesn't work try Method 1

ONLY IF all runs fine after Method 1: 
press CTRL+ALT+F1
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop

Then do what I suggested before to solve the problem with xorg

### Post by adamthompson on 2006-01-23
[QUOTE=tseliot]I need you to reproduce the error.
Therefore set the driver to "nvidia" in your xorg.conf
When GDM crashes at start-up Press CTRL+ALT+F1
Then type

Then:
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```

```
startx -- -verbose 5 -logverbose 5
```

You will get the same error.

sudo cp /var/log/Xorg.0.log /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log
(Of course you have to replace "your_username" with your username)

and post the content of that file (which you can find under /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log )


BTW if you want to access the GNOME or KDE again you can use this quick fix:
```
sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf
```
Set the driver back to "nv" instead of "nvidia"
CTRL+O to save
CTRL+X to exit

```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```

Then
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm start (if you use GDM)
```[/QUOTE]

### Post by tseliot on 2006-01-24
Type:
sudo passwd -l root

### Post by tseliot on 2006-01-24

### Post by tseliot on 2006-01-24
I can't find anything useful in the log file (maybe it's just me ).

I think you should ask for help on the Unofficial Nvidia Forums (in the Linux section).

Your card is not supported by Xorg 6.8.2 but with Xorg 6.9 and Xorg 7.0 (which is only in Ubuntu Dapper Drake) you won't have any problems. Dapper is not stable yet though. If you need a stable OS with Xorg 6.9 to wait until Dapper is out you might try PCLinux OS.

---

sudo rm /usr/src/nv/nvidia.ko

Then run the nvidia installer again (it should work this time)[/QUOTE]

```
 /usr/src/nv/nvidia.ko
```

but in

```
/lib/modules/2.6.12-10-386/volatile/nvidia.ko

### Post by tseliot on 2006-01-24
If you type:
sudo rm /lib/modules/2.6.12-10-386/volatile/nvidia.ko

Does the installer work?

### Post by BLTicklemonster on 2006-01-25

### Post by martinbriscoe on 2006-01-25
Arnieboy from the Automatix forum suggested I call in here for help - I have previously posted this but found for some reason I was in the AMD 64 area. So am reposting

My problem:

Running Automatix increased boot up time from 1 to 3 minutes. The computer hangs after it begins 'starting hardware abstraction layer' the screen goes blank apart from a white dash top left then after a minute or more the computer comes back to life and the ubuntu screen appears.

I have uninstalled most things using the advice in this (removing software installed by automatix) thread. Any idea whats going on and how to resolve the problem? 

I believe my card is: nvidia geforce mx/mx400 

Arnieboy said
>>>
yes this problem indeed is card related. I would suggest you refer to the following thread and ask for help. Tell them u have the nvidia-glx drivers in the repositories installed (which automatix installs.. nothing fancy other than that). mention your card details and they will help u on that further from there.
One of my team members maintains that thread.

Ok, let's try with the legacy driver, which perhaps it's more suitable for your card.

Open Terminal or Konsole and copy and paste the following command (as it is):
sudo apt-get install nvidia-glx-legacy linux-restricted-modules-`uname -r`-nvidia-legacy

it will ask you to remove nvidia-glx and perhaps the old restricted modules: say yes to that.

Then restart your computer and tell me if it solved the problem.

### Post by Stormbringer on 2006-01-26
Hi!

I finally overcame my lazyness and updated to the latest NVIDIA drivers by following your guide (eyecandy stuff here I come - thanks poof!). Worked like a charm. However, there is a little improvement to your great howto regarding installation on a AMD64 platform.

At the point where the installer asks about the installation of the 32-Bit OpenGL compatiblity libs I answered no - as advised by you. Afterwards I installed them by hand ... just in case I happen to cross any 32-Bit app that needs OpenGL (and having it accelerated would be a nice).

This step goes right between Step 12 and Step 13 in Method 2

12.1) Manually installing the 32-Bit OpenGL compatiblity libraries on AMD64

Extract the contents of the installer

```

# sudo sh NVIDIA-Linux-x86_64-1.0-8178-pkg2.run -x

```

This will create a directory called "NVIDIA-Linux-x86_64-1.0-8178-pkg2".

Now copy the 32-Bit OpenGL libraries to it's destination

```

# sudo cp -R ./NVIDIA-Linux-x86_64-1.0-8178-pkg2/usr/lib32/* /usr/lib32

```

Create the required symlinks

```

# cd /usr/lib32
# sudo ln -s libGL.so.1 libGL.so.1.0.8178
# sudo ln -s libGLcore.so.1 libGLcore.so.1.0.8178
# sudo ln -s libnvidia-cfg.so libnvidia-cfg.so.1.0.8178
# sudo ln -s libnvidia-cfg.so.1 libnvidia-cfg.so.1.0.8178
# sudo ln -s libnvidia-tls.so.1 libnvidia-tls.so.1.0.8178
# cd ./tls
# sudo ln -s libnvidia-tls-so.1 libnvidia-tls.so.1.0.8178
# sudo ldconfig

```

If you are using driver version 81.78 you can safely skip step 13 and proceed to step 14 - the "nvidia-settings" application has just been installed by the NVIDIA installer and should be available at "Applications" -> "System Tools" -> "NVIDIA X Server Settings".

NOTE: 32-Bit chroot - OpenGL acceleration?
I'm not sure about this - maybe someone likes to give it a try as I don't run any 32-Bit OpenGL applications inside the 32-Bit chroot.

Copy the aforementioned libraries together with its symlinks and the tls directory from /usr/lib32/ to /chroot/usr/lib/. When you are done open up the 32-Bit chroot Terminal and issue a "sudo ldconfig" to reflect the changes.

From my point of view this should enable hardware acceleration inside the 32-Bit chroot - but don't take it for granted.

Hope it's a valueable addition to the HOWTO.

Cheers, Storm.

---

[QUOTE=Stormbringer]Hi!

I finally overcame my lazyness and updated to the latest NVIDIA drivers by following your guide (eyecandy stuff here I come - thanks poof!). Worked like a charm. However, there is a little improvement to your great howto regarding installation on a AMD64 platform.

At the point where the installer asks about the installation of the 32-Bit OpenGL compatiblity libs I answered no - as advised by you. Afterwards I installed them by hand ... just in case I happen to cross any 32-Bit app that needs OpenGL (and having it accelerated would be a nice).

This step goes right between Step 12 and Step 13 in Method 2

12.1) Manually installing the 32-Bit OpenGL compatiblity libraries on AMD64

Extract the contents of the installer

```

# sudo sh NVIDIA-Linux-x86_64-1.0-8178-pkg2.run -x

```

This will create a directory called "NVIDIA-Linux-x86_64-1.0-8178-pkg2".

Now copy the 32-Bit OpenGL libraries to it's destination

```

# sudo cp -R ./NVIDIA-Linux-x86_64-1.0-8178-pkg2/usr/lib32/* /usr/lib32

```

Create the required symlinks

```

# cd /usr/lib32
# sudo ln -s libGL.so.1 libGL.so.1.0.8178
# sudo ln -s libGLcore.so.1 libGLcore.so.1.0.8178
# sudo ln -s libnvidia-cfg.so libnvidia-cfg.so.1.0.8178
# sudo ln -s libnvidia-cfg.so.1 libnvidia-cfg.so.1.0.8178
# sudo ln -s libnvidia-tls.so.1 libnvidia-tls.so.1.0.8178
# cd ./tls
# sudo ln -s libnvidia-tls-so.1 libnvidia-tls.so.1.0.8178
# sudo ldconfig

```

If you are using driver version 81.78 you can safely skip step 13 and proceed to step 14 - the "nvidia-settings" application has just been installed by the NVIDIA installer and should be available at "Applications" -> "System Tools" -> "NVIDIA X Server Settings".

NOTE: 32-Bit chroot - OpenGL acceleration?
I'm not sure about this - maybe someone likes to give it a try as I don't run any 32-Bit OpenGL applications inside the 32-Bit chroot.

Copy the aforementioned libraries together with its symlinks and the tls directory from /usr/lib32/ to /chroot/usr/lib/. When you are done open up the 32-Bit chroot Terminal and issue a "sudo ldconfig" to reflect the changes.

From my point of view this should enable hardware acceleration inside the 32-Bit chroot - but don't take it for granted.

Hope it's a valueable addition to the HOWTO.

Cheers, Storm.[/QUOTE]
Thanks for reporting. I'm not using Ubuntu 64bit any more so I'm not able to test it. I'll add it to the guide as soon as I have some spare time (I'm studying, as I always do ;)  )

sudo apt-get install nvidia-glx-legacy linux-restricted-modules-`uname -r`-nvidia-legacy

The solution (Thanks to MartinG) was to:
'sudo dpkg-reconfigure xserver-xorg'. This program selected the 'nv' driver and after rebooting the problem was solved. I understand that the nv driver may be a little basic but I dont play games so I dont think it will matter. 

I am sorry to have cross posted and to have taken up your time, but you guys  on these forums do a really great job, so many thanks

I'll probably be back ;)

Martin

### Post by Stormbringer on 2006-01-26
[QUOTE=Tibor60]
3. Then I tryed to do all by the method 2. Steps was going nicely until 
sudo sh NVIDIA-Linux-x86-1.0-8178-pkg1.run
The process finished with the error message:

What to do?[/QUOTE]

I think you either missed or overread a step in Method 2 ...

Method 2 / Step 2 advises you to do the following in a terminal:

```

# sudo apt-get install build-essential gcc gcc-3.4

```

This command will install all the tools you need to compile a sourcecode - as soon as they're installed we're still in the need for the kernel headers to satisfy the NVIDIA installer.

Method 2 / Step 5 cares about this ...

```

# sudo apt-get install linux-image-386 linux-headers-386

```

You may reduce the command to...

```

# sudo apt-get install linux-headers-`uname -r`

```

If you fire up the NVIDIA installer everything should go fine.

Carefully following the instructions line-by-line gets you to the goal of having the latest NVIDIA driver installed on your system ... it worked for me and it worked for others as well.

### Post by tseliot on 2006-01-26
[QUOTE=Tibor60]can I work with Ubuntu without going deeply in these things or better to go to an other OS?[/QUOTE]
If you don't use your graphic card to play games or need 3d acceleration then you can use the "nv" (i.e. the opensource driver) instead of "nvidia" (the proprietary one) and your system will work fine. There's no need to change distro (unless you dislike Ubuntu)

If you want to use 3d acceleration follow these steps:

sudo apt-get install linux-tree

cd /usr/src
sudo tar --bzip2 -xvf linux-source-2.6.12.tar.bz2
sudo ln -sf /usr/src/linux-source-2.6.12 /usr/src/linux
cd /usr/src/linux

Then
CTRL+ALT+F1
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop
OR
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm stop (if you use KDE)

CC=gcc-3.4
export CC

Then run the nvidia installer again in the following way:
cd path_to_your_nvidia_installer
sudo sh NVIDIA-Linux-x86-1.0-8178-pkg1.run [COLOR="Blue"]--kernel-source-path=/usr/src/linux[/COLOR]

Then tell me if it works

I did some experiments, and it seems that there is to version of errors:

"The kernel header file `/usr/src/linux/include/linux/version.h` does not exist. The most likely reason for this is that the kernel source files in `/usr/src/linux` have not been configured."

with this link the version.h file is found, but in that way I receive the first error message again:

"unable to load the kernel module `nvidia.ko` This is most likely because the kernel modul was built using the wrong kernel source file. ### Post by adamthompson on 2006-01-26
I got it working! I'm not quite sure what made the difference. In a PM, Iandefor suggested using VESA if I couldn't get the new NVIDIA driver to work. I did that and was able to get Gnome working although it looked bad (as though the refresh rate was set too low). But once I had Gnome and opened up Firefox, I realized that the network wasn't working. I managed to get the network working by looking at the Network Configuration section of /usr/share/doc/nforce/ReleaseNotes.html. Then I reinstalled the NVIDIA video driver and added Load "record" to the Modules section of xorg.conf. After that, it worked. I'm not quite sure which of the two things I did made a difference.

I'm happy now.

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-01-27
[QUOTE=Tibor60]Now i must go, but I will continue the "fight" in the evening.[/QUOTE]
Mmm... the installer doesn't seem to love you. You're not the only one. I've found a possible solution on the Nvidia forums.

sudo ln -sf /usr/src/linux-source-2.6.12 /usr/src/linux

su
cd /usr/src/linux
make clobber cloneconfig prepare-all

Then try the installer again (with the same option "--kernel-source-path" as before)

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-01-27
[QUOTE=Tibor60]I am not an adventure seeker, and of course I started with the method 1. All was going good, no error messages, not any problems in the process. The problem was only after re-starting the the PC. Not any NVIDIA splashscreens, not any 3D support... the system is not changed at all.[/QUOTE]

Well in that case you could do this after method 1:
sudo gedit /etc/X11/xorg.conf

Then set the driver from "nv" to "nvidia" in the the "Section Device".

The problem is that sometimes this command "sudo nvidia-glx-config enable" doesn't work (and doesn't set the driver from "nv" to "nvidia"). Therefore you have to do it manually.

I didn't understand what your real problem was with Method 1 in your 1st post. Sorry, but I have an exam next wednesday and I'm not at my 100% in these days ;) .

### Post by Tibor60 on 2006-01-27
I think there is problem with the kernel and kernel source, can you help me to re-install the right kernel sources? I did the steps, thinking that I need this 7174 driver:
sudo apt-get install nvidia-glx-legacy nvidia-settings linux-restricted-modules-`uname -r`-nvidia-legacy

sudo copy /etc/X11/xorg.conf /etc/X11/xorg.conf_backup
sudo nvidia-glx-config enable
 then I edited the NVIDIA-Settings.desktop file

restarted the X server... nothing, because the xorg.conf was without change.
Then I edited the xorg.conf file: isntead of "nv" I inserted "nvidia"
and deleted the "dri" and "GLcore" lines.

restarted the x server, anf it hanged on with the error:

"fatal: could not open /lib/modules/2.6.12-10-686/volatile/nvidia.ko
no such file or directory" 

What else?

### Post by tseliot on 2006-01-27
[QUOTE=Tibor60]Before to go farer, maybe it is important... I could check now some files on my broken winchester, and it seems that on the working  UHU linux system there was a special driver installed (and functioning well): NVIDIA-Linux-x86-1.0-7174-pkg1.run

I tried to install this driver, but there was an error message about the gcc versions. Maybe it can lead us to the solution?[/QUOTE]
The gcc problem is easy to solve:

sudo apt-get --purge remove nvidia-glx-legacy nvidia-settings linux-restricted-modules-`uname -r`-nvidia-legacy
sudo apt-get --purge remove nvidia-glx linux-restricted-modules-`uname -r`

then:
sudo apt-get install gcc-3.4 (just to be sure that you have it)
sudo ln -sf /usr/bin/gcc-3.4 /usr/bin/gcc

and run the installer

---

 Gdk-CRITICAL **: gdk_window_invalidate_rect: assertion ` window != NULL' failed

but the settings can be done, I hope I can ignore this error message.

### Post by tekwarren on 2006-01-28
I'm a newb and DID have some troubles getting the driver installed but I did get it in the end. Curiously though I ran into some issues: when I started the nvidia installer it said it could not detect a compatible precompiled kernal (and yes I did update it successfully as per the instructions). It asked me if I wanted to download one from nvidia's ftp which I tried and it said it failed. However I was somehow able to keep moving forward and it DID install the drivers successfully. I also did not have to do all the editing of the files to tell it to use glx or comment out the lines it says to in the instructions here...they where not even there. I also did not have to apt-get the nvidia "controll panel" it was just there when I restarted X. So I guess in the end it was pretty easy just confusing. Maybe the newer install package does alot of the last steps for you??

### Post by yetanothersteve on 2006-01-28
Thank you very much, I wanted to install the 1.0-8178 drivers from Nvidia and followed method 2. Tip: print the method you will use first or save the text to a text file and view under another console (CTRL-ALT-F2) for the non-X portions if you only have one computer. 

I had been running the Ubuntu NVidia packages for 1.0.7667 and wanted to see if there was a performance improvement with 8178. Quake IV demo now ran consistently in the 40 to 63fps range both indoors and outside as opposed to dropping into the 30s and even high 20s under the 7667 drivers.

I believe the NVidia driver package also included the NVidia Settings program as I was able to run 'nvidia-settings' without installing it. I look in Synaptic and it is clearly not installed.

I just bought an evga GeForce 6800XT and I think it is decent for the price.

### Post by adamthompson on 2006-01-28
I thought I had this working, but not quite. The only way I can get the new NVIDIA driver to work is to first use the VESA driver, then install the new NVIDIA driver, then type "/etc/init.d/gdm start". Once I restart the computer the xserver crashes at startup. Reinstalling the NVIDIA driver from the command line won't work. I have to edit /etc/X11/xorg.conf, changing "nvidia" to "vesa". Then I have to restart the computer and reinstall the NVIDIA driver, which will work until I restart the computer again.

### Post by tseliot on 2006-01-28
[QUOTE=adamthompson]I thought I had this working, but not quite. The only way I can get the new NVIDIA driver to work is to first use the VESA driver, then install the new NVIDIA driver, then type "/etc/init.d/gdm start". Once I restart the computer the xserver crashes at startup. Reinstalling the NVIDIA driver from the command line won't work. I have to edit /etc/X11/xorg.conf, changing "nvidia" to "vesa". Then I have to restart the computer and reinstall the NVIDIA driver, which will work until I restart the computer again.

Anybody have any idea what's going on here?[/QUOTE]
I need you to reproduce the error.
Therefore set the driver to "nvidia" in your xorg.conf
When GDM crashes at start-up Press CTRL+ALT+F1
Then type

Then:
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```

```
startx -- -verbose 5 -logverbose 5
```

You will get the same error.

sudo cp /var/log/Xorg.0.log /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log
(Of course you have to replace "your_username" with your username)

and post the content of that file (which you can find under /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log )


BTW if you want to access the GNOME or KDE again you can use this quick fix:
```
sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf
```
Set the driver back to "nv" instead of "nvidia"
CTRL+O to save
CTRL+X to exit

```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```

Then
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm start (if you use GDM)
```

---

here is the problem: nvidia-glx drivers as well as nvidia website (latest) drivers fail to start on boot. my monitor blanks out before X starts. i know i am installing them correctly, and by using the same methods i had in the past. I havent upgraded or changed any hardware, other than some hard drives. has there been some kind of update to the nvidia-glx package in the repo's? it is still 7667, so i assumed there wouldnt have been. 

anyway, here is my xorg.conf:

[quote=xorg.conf]# /etc/X11/xorg.conf (xorg X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.
#
# Edit this file with caution, and see the /etc/X11/xorg.conf manual page.
# (Type "man /etc/X11/xorg.conf" at the shell prompt.)
#
# This file is automatically updated on xserver-xorg package upgrades *only*
# if it has not been modified since the last upgrade of the xserver-xorg
# package.
#
# If you have edited this file but would like it to be automatically updated
# again, run the following command:
#   sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg

Section "Files"
    FontPath    "/usr/share/X11/fonts/misc"
    FontPath    "/usr/share/X11/fonts/cyrillic"
    FontPath    "/usr/share/X11/fonts/100dpi/:unscaled"
    FontPath    "/usr/share/X11/fonts/75dpi/:unscaled"
    FontPath    "/usr/share/X11/fonts/Type1"
    FontPath    "/usr/share/X11/fonts/CID"
    FontPath    "/usr/share/X11/fonts/100dpi"
    FontPath    "/usr/share/X11/fonts/75dpi"
        # paths to defoma fonts

    FontPath    "/var/lib/defoma/x-ttcidfont-conf.d/dirs/CID"
EndSection

Section "Module"
    Load    "GLcore"
    Load    "i2c"
    Load    "bitmap"
    Load    "ddc"
    Load    "dri"
    Load    "extmod"
    Load    "freetype"
    Load    "glx"
    Load    "int10"
    Load    "type1"
    Load    "vbe"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier    "Generic Keyboard"
    Driver        "kbd"
    Option        "CoreKeyboard"
    Option        "XkbRules"    "xorg"
    Option        "XkbModel"    "pc104"
    Option        "XkbLayout"    "us"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier    "Configured Mouse"
    Driver        "mouse"
    Option        "CorePointer"
    Option        "Device"        "/dev/input/mice"
    Option        "Protocol"        "ImPS/2"
    Option        "Emulate3Buttons"    "true"
    Option        "ZAxisMapping"        "4 5"
EndSection

Section "Device"
    Identifier    "NVIDIA Corporation NV40 [GeForce 6800 Ultra/GeForce 6800 GT]"
    Driver        "nv"
    BusID        "PCI:1:0:0"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
    Identifier    "Generic Monitor"
    Option        "DPMS"
    HorizSync    28-64
    VertRefresh    43-60
EndSection

Section "Screen"
    Identifier    "Default Screen"
    Device        "NVIDIA Corporation NV40 [GeForce 6800 Ultra/GeForce 6800 GT]"
    Monitor        "Generic Monitor"
    DefaultDepth    24
    SubSection "Display"
        Depth        1
        Modes        "1280x1024" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection "Display"
        Depth        4
        Modes        "1280x1024" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection "Display"
        Depth        8
        Modes        "1280x1024" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection "Display"
        Depth        15
        Modes        "1280x1024" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection "Display"
        Depth        16
        Modes        "1280x1024" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection "Display"
        Depth        24
        Modes        "1280x1024" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
EndSection

Section "ServerLayout"
    Identifier    "Default Layout"
    Screen        "Default Screen"
    InputDevice    "Generic Keyboard"
    InputDevice    "Configured Mouse"
EndSection

Section "DRI"
    Mode    0666
EndSection
[/quote] 

thanks for reading, i hope it's just some stupid mistake i am making somewhere, but i could almost garauntee that i have been doing it the same way for as long as i have been using breezy :-k

### Post by tseliot on 2006-01-28
[QUOTE=superhew]hey

sorry if i am asking a question already answered, but I couldn't find anything in the search...

i have been using breezy (both 32 and 64 bit versions) since it's release, and have been able to run the nvidia-glx drivers from the repo's without a problem. Recently i had all of my linux distros crash, all booting up with problems and failures. i completely formatted all my hard drives, and im starting off again from scratch. 

here is the problem: nvidia-glx drivers as well as nvidia website (latest) drivers fail to start on boot. my monitor blanks out before X starts. i know i am installing them correctly, and by using the same methods i had in the past. I havent upgraded or changed any hardware, other than some hard drives. has there been some kind of update to the nvidia-glx package in the repo's? it is still 7667, so i assumed there wouldnt have been. 

anyway, here is my xorg.conf:

 

thanks for reading, i hope it's just some stupid mistake i am making somewhere, but i could almost garauntee that i have been doing it the same way for as long as i have been using breezy :-k[/QUOTE]
I can't notice anything wrong in your xorg.conf. 
I need you to reproduce the error.
Therefore set the driver to "nvidia" in your xorg.conf
When GDM crashes at start-up Press CTRL+ALT+F1
Then type

Then:
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```

```
startx -- -verbose 5 -logverbose 5
```

You will get the same error.

sudo cp /var/log/Xorg.0.log /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log
(Of course you have to replace "your_username" with your username)

and post the content of that file (which you can find under /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log )


BTW if you want to access the GNOME or KDE again you can use this quick fix:
```
sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf
```
Set the driver back to "nv" instead of "nvidia"
CTRL+O to save
CTRL+X to exit

```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```

Then
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm start (if you use GDM)
```

---

### Post by chandra on 2006-01-28
Very good HOW TO.  Thanks.

I followed Method 1 with no hitches.  I have one question.  The lines
 
#Load "dri"
#Load “GLcore”

are [COLOR="Blue"]not commented[/COLOR] out in my [COLOR="Red"]modified[/COLOR] /etc/X11/xorg.conf file after I run
 
sudo nvidia-glx-config enable

Is this correct behaviour, or do I have to manually edit the file?

I also have one comment: the line 

sudo kate /usr/share/applications/NVIDIA-Settings.desktop

in your HOW To should be

kdesu kate /usr/share/applications/NVIDIA-Settings.desktop

for it to work properly.

### Post by tseliot on 2006-01-29
> **chandra]Very good HOW TO.  Thanks.

I followed Method 1 with no hitches.  I have one question.  The lines
 
#Load "dri"
#Load &#8220 said:**
> not commented[/COLOR] out in my [COLOR="Red"]modified[/COLOR] /etc/X11/xorg.conf file after I run
 
sudo nvidia-glx-config enable

Is this correct behaviour, or do I have to manually edit the file?
I usually don't comment them out but some users have problems if those lines are enabled. If it works for you you can keep them as they are.

[QUOTE=chandra]I also have one comment: the line 

sudo kate /usr/share/applications/NVIDIA-Settings.desktop

in your HOW To should be

kdesu kate /usr/share/applications/NVIDIA-Settings.desktop

for it to work properly.[/QUOTE]
Well, I don't use KDE in Ubuntu but when I did "sudo kate" worked for me. Anyhow if "kdesu kate" works better I might change the guide.

### Post by skwid on 2006-01-29
Sorry to bother but I did a fresh install of breezy.  I have the newest nvidia drivers.  I followed step 2 to the letter and the nvidia installer still tells me that it can't locate the kernel source.   Is there another way?

uname -r gets me 2.6.12-10-386

I have verified that all of those linux headers and modules are loaded.  

I've done this before but I'm stumped here.

Thanks.

--EDIT--

Nevermind I had to do export CC=/usr/bin/gcc-3.4
and it worked fine then...

Jeremy

### Post by jtpratt on 2006-01-30
[QUOTE=tseliot]That's right, the installation of a new card is not covered by the guide yet.

Try this: 
boot in Recovery mode
```
dpkg-reconfigure xserver-org
```
select you videocard and the "nv" (driver for now). Whenever you find a question for which you don't know the answer just press ENTER.
Another thing: if you have to enable the right resolution you have to do it with the spacebar (then you can press ENTER).

Then reboot:
```
reboot
```

Access your BIOS and disable your Integrated graphic card. Perhaps you can choose between PCI and AGP (well, I don't know your BIOS).

Then boot Ubuntu.

If you manage to use the graphic card then you can try to install the nvidia drivers.

About the DVI: there are some users who have problems with the drivers. In that case a VGA cable (or an adapter) is the solution.



You're not the 1st user who has this problem. It's the official method.
In that case you have to type:
sudo gedit /etc/X11/xorg.conf
or
sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf

and set the driver from "nv" to "nvidia"


Have a look at point 3 of the PROBLEMS SECTION.[/QUOTE]

### Post by tseliot on 2006-01-30
[QUOTE=jtpratt]I tried what you said...I was able to boot into gnome using 'nv' instead of 'nvidia' once I knew that my PCI setting was 1:9:0.  Then I tried the nvidia installer again with method 2 instructions and it failed again.  I get the "no matching precompiled interface" and "gcc-version check failed", "./usr/src/bv/conftest.sh:line 19 gcc-3.3 command not found".

I did which gcc and sudo ln -s /usr/bin/gcc /usr/bin/cc like in your problems sections, and it still didn't work.  Here's a copy of the nvidia-installer log, can you help?[/QUOTE]
Try this:
sudo apt-get install gcc-3.4 (just to be sure that you have it)
sudo ln -sf /usr/bin/gcc-3.4 /usr/bin/gcc

and run the installer (it should work this time)

### Post by jtpratt on 2006-01-30
[QUOTE=tseliot]Try this:
sudo apt-get install gcc-3.4 (just to be sure that you have it)
sudo ln -sf /usr/bin/gcc-3.4 /usr/bin/gcc

and run the installer (it should work this time)[/QUOTE]
I made the link to gcc-3.4 and ran the nvidia installer again.  It said it could not find kernal-source.  I installed linux-headers-2.6.12-10-386 and it ran and installed successfully.  It also updated xorg.conf successfully using driver 'nvidia', etc.  On reboot, the xserver fails to start.  Both nvidia settings and nvidia x server settings are installed in my applications -> system menu.  

Here is the results of my /var/log/nvidia-installer.log file, what can I try now?
