### Post by tseliot on 2006-01-31
> **OPaul]When I run the Nvidia installer I get the following error said:**
> 
Why do you need to use 5336? Did you try Method 1 with the Legacy driver (7174)?

[QUOTE=OPaul]Also, what is the difference between using the nv driver and the nvidia driver in xorg.conf?
nvidia driver (the proprietary driver) enables 3d acceleration which "nv" (the open source driver) doesn't.

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-01-31
[QUOTE=jtpratt]I made the link to gcc-3.4 and ran the nvidia installer again.  It said it could not find kernal-source.  I installed linux-headers-2.6.12-10-386 and it ran and installed successfully.  It also updated xorg.conf successfully using driver 'nvidia', etc.  On reboot, the xserver fails to start.  Both nvidia settings and nvidia x server settings are installed in my applications -> system menu.  

Here is the results of my /var/log/nvidia-installer.log file, what can I try now?[/QUOTE]
The installation went fine.
Now I need you to reproduce the error.
Therefore set the driver to "nvidia" in your xorg.conf
When GDM (or kdm) crashes at start-up Press CTRL+ALT+F1
Then type

Then:
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```

```
startx -- -verbose 5 -logverbose 5
```

You will get the same error.

sudo cp /var/log/Xorg.0.log /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log
(Of course you have to replace "your_username" with your username)

and post the content of that file (which you can find under /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log )


BTW if you want to access the GNOME or KDE again you can use this quick fix:
```
sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf
```
Set the driver back to "nv" instead of "nvidia"
CTRL+O to save
CTRL+X to exit

```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```

Then
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm start (if you use GDM)
```

---

### Post by OPaul on 2006-01-31
> **tseliot]Why do you need to use 5336? Did you try Method 1 with the Legacy driver (7174)?
[/QUOTE]

Drivers past 5336 cause a black bar to appear on the right side of certain Toshiba laptops. See the following URLs said:**
> http://www.laptopvideo2go.com/wiki/index.php/Trouble_Shooting#Toshiba_.26_Black_Bar_Displays[/url]
[http://www.laptopvideo2go.com/forum/index.php?showtopic=5832](http://www.laptopvideo2go.com/forum/index.php?showtopic=5832)

The cause is because the wrong EDID information is being sent after Nvidia changed something in the drivers at some point causing the information to be read incorrectly... or something like that.

I have a Geforce 420 Go which is not supported by the legacy drivers. When I did try them, if I remember correctly, Gnome wouldn't load at all.

[QUOTE]
nvidia driver (the proprietary driver) enables 3d acceleration which "nv" (the open source driver) doesn't.
Ah, because the nv driver works fine... no black bar. But I sort of need that 3d acceleration part. For one thing, I can't play movies full screen with the nv driver.

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-01-31
[QUOTE=OPaul]The cause is because the wrong EDID information is being sent after Nvidia changed something in the drivers at some point causing the information to be read incorrectly... or something like that.[/QUOTE]
You can disable EDID but first of all you need to get the driver to work.

[QUOTE=OPaul]I have a Geforce 420 Go which is not supported by the legacy drivers. When I did try them, if I remember correctly, Gnome wouldn't load at all.[/QUOTE]
I've found this on the Unofficial nvidia forums:
```
0175GeForce4 420 Go [NV17M] nVidia-Driver-7174-and-7676
0176GeForce4 420 Go 32M [NV17M] nVidia-Driver-7174-and-7676
```
This means that your card is supported by both 7174 and the latest driver (8178)
Try 8178 and tell me how it goes

[QUOTE=OPaul]For one thing, I can't play movies full screen with the nv driver.[/QUOTE]
Which player are you using? If it's Mplayer it doesn't depend on the graphic driver (have a look at a guide about Mplayer)

### Post by tseliot on 2006-01-31
[QUOTE=OPaul]I know the card is supported, the card isn't the issue. It's the laptop, something to do with it's monitor and the BIOS. The 8178 wouldn't even load Gnome, i just got a blank screen with an underscore.

I know the 5336 drivers will work, I just don't understand what's wrong with the installer.[/QUOTE]
I can't help you with drivers 5336, it seems to be trying to compile things which are deprecated. 

However if you try 8178 and reproduce the error I can help you to solve it.
If so, do this thing:
Install driver 8178
Reboot
When GDM (or kdm) crashes at start-up Press CTRL+ALT+F1
Then type

Then:
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```

```
startx -- -verbose 5 -logverbose 5
```

You will get the same error.

sudo cp /var/log/Xorg.0.log /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log
(Of course you have to replace "your_username" with your username)

and post the content of that file (which you can find under /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log )


BTW if you want to access the GNOME or KDE again you can use this quick fix:
```
sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf
```
Set the driver back to "nv" instead of "nvidia"
CTRL+O to save
CTRL+X to exit

```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```

Then
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm start (if you use GDM)
```

### Post by jinacio on 2006-01-31
I actually had the exact same problem...

short-term fix was to add the line:
```

Option          "NvAGP" "0" 

```
to the device section of xorg.conf ("1" works aswell)
this would disable the use of agpgart and let me get into X, although without any agp support.

then i got lucky and got a new bios for my board (asus A8V dlx, via chipset...) and now everything is working fine.

hope it helps somehow...

---

### Post by nemrod on 2006-01-31
[QUOTE=tseliot]1) Post the content of your /etc/X11/xorg.conf
2) Which method did you use to install the drivers?
3) What's the model of your card?
4) Do you use Ubuntu 32bit or Ubuntu 64bit?[/QUOTE]
1.```
Section "ServerLayout"
    Identifier     "Default Layout"
    Screen         "Default Screen" 0 0
    InputDevice    "Generic Keyboard"
    InputDevice    "Configured Mouse" "CorePointer"
EndSection

Section "Files"
    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/misc"
    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/cyrillic"
    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/100dpi/:unscaled"
    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/75dpi/:unscaled"
    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/Type1"
    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/CID"
    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/100dpi"
    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/75dpi"
    FontPath        "/var/lib/defoma/x-ttcidfont-conf.d/dirs/TrueType"
    FontPath        "/var/lib/defoma/x-ttcidfont-conf.d/dirs/CID"
EndSection

Section "Module"
    Load           "i2c"
    Load           "bitmap"
    Load           "dbe"
    Load           "ddc"
    Load           "extmod"
    Load           "freetype"
    Load           "glx"
    Load           "int10"
    Load           "record"
    Load           "type1"
    Load           "vbe"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier     "Generic Keyboard"
    Driver         "kbd"
    Option         "CoreKeyboard"
    Option         "XkbRules" "xorg"
    Option         "XkbModel" "pc105"
    Option         "XkbLayout" "se"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier     "Configured Mouse"
    Driver         "mouse"
    Option         "Protocol" "evdev"
    Option         "Dev Name" "Logitech USB-PS/2 Optical Mouse"
    Option         "Dev Phys" "usb-0000:00:10.1-2/input0"
    Option         "Buttons" "10"
    Option         "ZAxisMapping" "4 5"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
    Identifier     "Generic Monitor"
    HorizSync       28.0 - 64.0
    VertRefresh     43.0 - 60.0
    Option         "DPMS"
EndSection

Section "Device"
    Identifier     "Generic Video Card"
    Driver         "nvidia"
    BusID          "PCI:1:0:0"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
    Identifier     "Default Screen"
    Device         "Generic Video Card"
    Monitor        "Generic Monitor"
    DefaultDepth    24
    Option         "NoLogo"
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       24
        Modes      "1280x1024" "1024x768"
    EndSubSection
EndSection
```
2. Method 2
3. 6600GT
4. 32bit (on 64bit CPU)

I appreciate the help, thanks :)

The installation worked both ways, yet both are giving me the same problem. 

When I boot up, as soon as I see the nvida splash screen, the screen no longer fills up my entire monitor. The right hand side of the monitor is simple vertical colored lines (no desktop display) and the bottom is just the top of my desktop (like it is wrapping around). 

I refered to the xorg.conf manual at [http://wiki.x.org/X11R6.8.0/doc/xorg.conf.5.html](http://wiki.x.org/X11R6.8.0/doc/xorg.conf.5.html) .

I have a similar, if not the exact same, as you. I have a Toshiba laptop with the 420 Go and when I install any Nvidia driver after 55.xx a black vertical line appears down the right side of the monitor (I know it starts after 55.xx because it was the same in Windows). The cause, from what I understand, is incorrect information is being sent via EDID. Here are some links to pages that I am currently using to fix the problem.

[http://www.laptopvideo2go.com/wiki/index.php/Trouble_Shooting#Toshiba_.26_Black_Bar_Displays](http://www.laptopvideo2go.com/wiki/index.php/Trouble_Shooting#Toshiba_.26_Black_Bar_Displays)
[http://www.laptopvideo2go.com/forum/index.php?showtopic=8667](http://www.laptopvideo2go.com/forum/index.php?showtopic=8667)
[http://en.tldp.org/HOWTO/XFree86-Video-Timings-HOWTO/fixes.html#AEN448](http://en.tldp.org/HOWTO/XFree86-Video-Timings-HOWTO/fixes.html#AEN448)
[http://download.nvidia.com/XFree86/Linux-x86/1.0-8178/README/appendix-i.html](http://download.nvidia.com/XFree86/Linux-x86/1.0-8178/README/appendix-i.html)

For me, the solution should be easy. I just need to install an older driver, 5336, but the Nvidia installer fails. You may want to try this, you may have better luck.

- EDIT -

Add the following under the "Screen" section to override the information being sent from the BIOS.
```
Option   "IgnoreEDID" "true"
```

After looking at your config file, I incorporated the HorizSync values you had and added the IgnoreEDID option and ... problem solved.

---

### Post by jacrider on 2006-01-31
I successfully installed the 8178 drivers from the nvidia site using method 2.  I am running the latest 386 kernel and the card is a nvidia GeForce FX 5500 in an older AGP slot.  No problems in Windows either.

Then ... I rebooted a couple of days later and x won't start.  Only going in to edit the xorg.conf file to remove "nvidia" and replace with "nv" seems to get me back and running, but not with the proper support.

### Post by jtpratt on 2006-01-31
[QUOTE=tseliot]The installation went fine.
Now I need you to reproduce the error.
Therefore set the driver to "nvidia" in your xorg.conf
When GDM (or kdm) crashes at start-up Press CTRL+ALT+F1
Then type

Then:
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```

```
startx -- -verbose 5 -logverbose 5
```

You will get the same error.

sudo cp /var/log/Xorg.0.log /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log
(Of course you have to replace "your_username" with your username)

and post the content of that file (which you can find under /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log )


BTW if you want to access the GNOME or KDE again you can use this quick fix:
```
sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf
```
Set the driver back to "nv" instead of "nvidia"
CTRL+O to save
CTRL+X to exit

```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```

Then
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm start (if you use GDM)
```[/QUOTE]

### Post by brharri1 on 2006-01-31
> 
**Originally posted by OPaul**

For me, the solution should be easy. I just need to install an older driver, 5336, but the Nvidia installer fails. You may want to try this, you may have better luck.

The installer also fails when I try it. 

> Add the following under the "Screen" section to override the information being sent from the BIOS.
Code:

Option "IgnoreEDID" "true"


After looking at your config file, I incorporated the HorizSync values you had and added the IgnoreEDID option and ... problem solved.

I tried this, and the only thing on the monitor was a faint horizontal bar across the screen. When I rebooted again X failed to start. 

Thanks for the quick reply

---

8178 - freezing the pc right after boot...

### Post by tseliot on 2006-02-01
[QUOTE=jacrider]I successfully installed the 8178 drivers from the nvidia site using method 2.  I am running the latest 386 kernel and the card is a nvidia GeForce FX 5500 in an older AGP slot.  No problems in Windows either.

Then ... I rebooted a couple of days later and x won't start.  Only going in to edit the xorg.conf file to remove "nvidia" and replace with "nv" seems to get me back and running, but not with the proper support.

I have tried everything I can find to address this, but I can't seem to make any progress.  Any help would be greatly appreciated.

Andrew.[/QUOTE]
Ok, I've got a (naive) question for you: Did you try to reinstall the driver?
Please do it, I'll explain you the reason later.

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-02-01
[QUOTE=OPaul]Was this a fix for 8178 or the black bar on all releases after 55.xx?[/QUOTE]
No, this option (which is in the Problems section of my guide) only disables the AGP but sometimes avoids lock-ups. 

Sorry but yesterday I was too tired to think (I've just come back from an exam :) )

### Post by Havoc on 2006-02-01
[norelation]Does anyone know if NVAGP has any advantages over AGPGART, and if so, how to enable it?[/norelation]

Here's my /etc/modules:

```
# /etc/modules: kernel modules to load at boot time.
#
# This file contains the names of kernel modules that should be loaded
# at boot time, one per line. Lines beginning with "#" are ignored.

lp
mousedev
psmouse
nvidia_agp
nvidia NVreg_EnableAGPSBA=1 NVreg_EnableAGPFW=1

```

and, I've put the "NvAGP" "1" option in my xorg.conf, but it either reverts back to AGPGART or says that "No AGP driver found" or whatever.

So? Anyone?

Thanks. ;)

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-02-01
[QUOTE=brharri1]I am having problems with the nvidia driver on my dell laptop Geforce4 440 Go. I had previously installed the drivers via the repositories (nvidia-glx), and have since reinstalled the drivers using Method 2 on the initial post, to the letter. The installation worked both ways, yet both are giving me the same problem. 

When I boot up, as soon as I see the nvida splash screen, the screen no longer fills up my entire monitor. The right hand side of the monitor is simple vertical colored lines (no desktop display) and the bottom is just the top of my desktop (like it is wrapping around). 

I refered to the xorg.conf manual at [http://wiki.x.org/X11R6.8.0/doc/xorg.conf.5.html](http://wiki.x.org/X11R6.8.0/doc/xorg.conf.5.html) .

Here is what my xorg.conf looks like: 



In Section "Monitor",  the line "DisplaySize     200 200" was not originally there. I added that after reading the manual, and tried several different values, but it did not change anything. 

This forum has always helped me solve every problem before, even though this is my first post. Does anyone have any suggestions?

Thanks in advance,

Brian[/QUOTE]
Try the suggestions in point 4 of the "Problems Section" of my guide and tell me if they solve the problem.

### Post by tseliot on 2006-02-01
[QUOTE=Havoc][norelation]Does anyone know if NVAGP has any advantages over AGPGART, and if so, how to enable it?[/norelation]

Here's my /etc/modules:

```
# /etc/modules: kernel modules to load at boot time.
#
# This file contains the names of kernel modules that should be loaded
# at boot time, one per line. Lines beginning with "#" are ignored.

lp
mousedev
psmouse
nvidia_agp
nvidia NVreg_EnableAGPSBA=1 NVreg_EnableAGPFW=1

```

and, I've put the "NvAGP" "1" option in my xorg.conf, but it either reverts back to AGPGART or says that "No AGP driver found" or whatever.

So? Anyone?

Thanks. ;)[/QUOTE]
You should use which works best for you (i.e. if it doesn't cause you problems don't touch it)

Anyhow you can post the output of:
```
cat /proc/driver/nvidia/agp/status
```

### Post by jacrider on 2006-02-01
From tseliot:

> Ok, I've got a (naive) question for you: Did you try to reinstall the driver?
Please do it, I'll explain you the reason later.

I did reinstall the drivers (and removed and reinstalled nvidia-settings).  This involved recompiling as well.
Of course it now seems to work ... why?  What did I do wrong the first time or how have I messed them up?

Many thanks for your assistance.  This HOWTO is really good.

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-02-01
[QUOTE=jacrider]From tseliot:



I did reinstall the drivers (and removed and reinstalled nvidia-settings).  This involved recompiling as well.
Of course it now seems to work ... why?  What did I do wrong the first time or how have I messed them up?

Many thanks for your assistance.  This HOWTO is really good.[/QUOTE]
Thanks for appreciating my work.

I think your kernel was updated (when you got the latest kernel) and the driver needs to be reinstalled everytime you change or upgrade your kernel.

---

### Post by jacrider on 2006-02-01
Thanks.  So this means that each time the Update Manager does a kernel update, we will need to re-install the nvidia drivers?  That's ok as it really isn't that hard, just want to make sure I remember to do it.

Again, thanks for your assistance.

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-02-01
[QUOTE=jacrider]Thanks.  So this means that each time the Update Manager does a kernel update, we will need to re-install the nvidia drivers?[/QUOTE]

That's right. I should say it clearly in my guide.

---

### Post by Havoc on 2006-02-02
[QUOTE=tseliot]You should use which works best for you (i.e. if it doesn't cause you problems don't touch it)

Anyhow you can post the output of:
```
cat /proc/driver/nvidia/agp/status
```[/QUOTE]

Sorry for being late in the game (again), but here is the output:

```
Status:          Enabled
Driver:          AGPGART
AGP Rate:        8x
Fast Writes:     Enabled
SBA:             Enabled

```

NOTE: I've already done this.It either says AGPGART, or (When I enable "NVAGP" "1" in the xorg.conf) it says "error, you haven't loaded AGP module" or something on those lines.

I wouldn't post this if I hadn't already searched for an answer.The module nvidia_agp *does* exist.It *is* in my /etc/modules, yet it is not loaded, or not recognised by X.Oh, and I'm using the latest drivers (1.0-8178 ).

### Post by tseliot on 2006-02-02
[QUOTE=Havoc]Sorry for being late in the game (again), but here is the output:

```
Status:          Enabled
Driver:          AGPGART
AGP Rate:        8x
Fast Writes:     Enabled
SBA:             Enabled

```

NOTE: I've already done this.It either says AGPGART, or (When I enable "NVAGP" "1" in the xorg.conf) it says "error, you haven't loaded AGP module" or something on those lines.

I wouldn't post this if I hadn't already searched for an answer.The module nvidia_agp *does* exist.It *is* in my /etc/modules, yet it is not loaded, or not recognised by X.Oh, and I'm using the latest drivers (1.0-8178 ). 

:-k[/QUOTE]
I still don't understand why you hate agpgart so much :p 

Anyhow I think you can find the solution among the following options (of course you can use ONLY 1 per time):
```
Option                      Effect
Option "NvAgp" "0"      disables AGP support
Option "NvAgp" "1"      use NVAGP, if possible
Option "NvAgp" "2"      use AGPGART, if possible
Option "NvAGP" "3"      try AGPGART; if that fails, try NVAGP
```

If there isn't an option that suits your needs I'm afraid you will have to learn to live with AGPGART.

---

### Post by Havoc on 2006-02-02
Hey, thanks for the quick answer.

The reason I so hate AGPGART is because I was under the belief that it's slower than using NVAGP. ;) 

Well, I read that chart on the nvidia website a couple of months ago.Right now I use "NVAGP" "3" because "1" gives me problems.If there are no advantages in using NVAGP, then I'm better off using AGPGART.

Well, if someone confirms the fact that NVAGP is not any much better than AGPGART, I'll stop annoying you.But until then....I'll just post some completely gratuitous pictures of penises to hopefully annoy and spark some sort of controversy.

\\:D/

---

### Post by deadlydeathcone on 2006-02-02
Has anyone had any luck yet compiling the latest nvidia drivers in kernel 2.6.15? I'm using Dapper and compiled using gcc-4.0 but haven't had any luck getting X to recognize the driver as of yet.

I'm probably just being picky as the version 8874 drivers in the repository work just fine, but the newest work much better with xcompmgr.

Thanks in advance.

edit: Nevermind. I tried setting the CC to either 4.0 or 3.4, then tried redirecting 4.0 to 3.4 for the install, all without success. It looks like this may be a problem with the 2.6.15 kernel that I can't really justify taking the time to fix for a slightly more stable composite. No biggy.

### Post by ivuntu on 2006-02-03
\\:D/ Thanks a lot for this guide!
It worked for me, I have a nvidia 6600 GT card. I just upgraded from Hoary to Breezy, and my screen kept freezing on me with the old NVidia drivers. 

Only thing unexpected was that when I ran the installer, it gave a message that it could not fount a precompiled kernel interface. It tried dowloading this kernel-interface-thing, but it could not find one for my kernel, so it build one itself. 
This seemed to work.The rest went very smoothly. I suppose this is very obivious for most users, but as a linux newbie, I am still in the 
' I-just-type-in-what-it-says-on-the-screen' phase.

Thanks again!

---

### Post by ivuntu on 2006-02-03
:( I was "cheering too early"  (Dutch expression):

the updating process went well (method 2), or so I thought (see earlier post); but suddenly the monitor went blank. crtl-alt-f1 or ctrl-alt-delete etc. did nothing, so I pressed reset. 

then X did not start: 
error: NVidia kernel module is version: 1-0-7667 which does not match the X module version: 1.0.8178 (the version I downloaded from NVidia.com). So I am back to a generic Vesa now, because the nv (open source) did not work for me as well. 

If I understand this correctly, I compiled something in the kernel with a wrong version. 

Any suggestions are most welcome!!!

Thanks in advance!

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-02-03
[QUOTE=ivuntu]:( I was "cheering too early"  (Dutch expression):

the updating process went well (method 2), or so I thought (see earlier post); but suddenly the monitor went blank. crtl-alt-f1 or ctrl-alt-delete etc. did nothing, so I pressed reset. 

then X did not start: 
error: NVidia kernel module is version: 1-0-7667 which does not match the X module version: 1.0.8178 (the version I downloaded from NVidia.com). So I am back to a generic Vesa now, because the nv (open source) did not work for me as well. 

If I understand this correctly, I compiled something in the kernel with a wrong version. 

Any suggestions are most welcome!!!

Thanks in advance![/QUOTE]
I need you to reproduce the error.
Therefore set the driver to "nvidia" in your xorg.conf
When GDM (or kdm) crashes at start-up Press CTRL+ALT+F1
Then type

Then:
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```

```
startx -- -verbose 5 -logverbose 5
```

You will get the same error.

sudo cp /var/log/Xorg.0.log /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log
(Of course you have to replace "your_username" with your username)

and post the content of that file (which you can find under /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log )


BTW if you want to access the GNOME or KDE again you can use this quick fix:
```
sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf
```
Set the driver back to "nv" instead of "nvidia"
CTRL+O to save
CTRL+X to exit

```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```

Then
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm start (if you use GDM)
```

### Post by mo_x on 2006-02-03
@ivuntu
Did you remove (with --purge option) the nvidia-glx and linux-restricted-modules packages before running the nvidia installer script?

---

### Post by ivuntu on 2006-02-03
Hi mo_x,

[QUOTE=mo_x]@ivuntu
Did you remove (with --purge option) the nvidia-glx and linux-restricted-modules packages before running the nvidia installer script?[/QUOTE]


Yes, I did, but I got the message that the packages were not there. (steps 7,8 and 9 of method 2).

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-02-03
[QUOTE=ivuntu]Hi mo_x,




Yes, I did, but I got the message that the packages were not there. (steps 7,8 and 9 of method 2).[/QUOTE]
Post the output of the following command:
locate nvidia.ko

---

### Post by ivuntu on 2006-02-03
[QUOTE=tseliot]Post the output of the following command:
locate nvidia.ko[/QUOTE]

here's what I get:
```
/lib/modules/2.6.10-5-386/kernel/drivers/video/nvidia.ko
/lib/modules/2.6.10-6-386/kernel/drivers/video/nvidia.ko

```

So it is there alright, but  I checked it again: even if I remove and purge the Nvidia glx and settings and linux-restricted-modules, this file nvidia.ko still exists.

So what do I do now? remove this file also? If so, please tell me how, as I get an error when I try.

---

### Post by jtpratt on 2006-02-03
[QUOTE=tseliot]Try the options suggested in Point 4 of the Problems Section of my guide and tell me how it goes.[/QUOTE]
it says that "NVIDIA kernel module is 1.0.7667 and this X module is 1.0.8178" and that's the problem.  How do we get them the same version?  Did the official nvidia installer screw up when installing?

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-02-03
[QUOTE=ivuntu]So it is there alright, but  I checked it again: even if I remove and purge the Nvidia glx and settings and linux-restricted-modules, this file nvidia.ko still exists.

So what do I do now? remove this file also? If so, please tell me how, as I get an error when I try.[/QUOTE]

[QUOTE=jtpratt]it says that "NVIDIA kernel module is 1.0.7667 and this X module is 1.0.8178" and that's the problem. How do we get them the same version? Did the official nvidia installer screw up when installing?[/QUOTE]

Both of you, could you try this?:
sudo cp /boot/initrd.img-`uname -r` ~
sudo mkinitrd -o /boot/initrd.img-`uname -r`

Then set your driver to nvidia in your xorg.conf (if it's already set to nvidia leave it as it is) and restart your computer.

Please, tell me if it works

---

### Post by ivuntu on 2006-02-03
[QUOTE=tseliot]Both of you, could you try this?:
sudo cp /boot/initrd.img-`uname -r` ~
sudo mkinitrd -o /boot/initrd.img-`uname -r`

Then set your driver to nvidia in your xorg.conf (if it's already set to nvidia leave it as it is) and restart your computer.

Please, tell me if it works[/QUOTE]

For me, X still doesn't start, it seems like it cannot find the GLX module. Here's the log:

### Post by tseliot on 2006-02-03
[QUOTE=ivuntu]For me, X still doesn't start, it seems like it cannot find the GLX module. Here's the log:[/QUOTE]
Press CTRL+ALT+F1
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop
cd path_to_the_nvidia_installer
sudo sh name_of_the_nvidia_installer --uninstall
sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf

And set the driver to "vesa" or "nv"

then type:
sudo reboot

After you boot in Ubuntu follow method 2 and try to install the driver again.

---

I cannot run the sh script from method 2 it says I have no precompiled kernal interface.   I looked at the /usr/src and saw no source file (just headers)  

The monitor is the viewsonice va2012wb (costco special).  

With the NV driver I get 1600 X 1200 res.  



I can't get the DVI to work at all (just says "no signal") so, now I am on analog.

Anyway I really just want to get the DVI to work and be able to watch WM9 on xine which, by the way, isn't working now either.

### Post by tseliot on 2006-02-04
[QUOTE=yonkman]I cannot run the sh script from method 2 it says I have no precompiled kernal interface.   I looked at the /usr/src and saw no source file (just headers) [/QUOTE]
That's ok and the nvidia installer should attempt to compile the module. Could you post your nvidia log file (I don't remember the exact name) which you can find in  /var/log/ ?

Oh, and please remove this option: Option"UseInternalAGPGART" "no"

[QUOTE=yonkman]I can't get the DVI to work at all (just says "no signal") so, now I am on analog.[/QUOTE]
I suppose you didn't install Ubuntu with your DVI cable plugged in. In theory you could reconfigure the xserver (sudo dpkg-reconfigure xserver-xorg) and fix the problem. However I think that you should reinstall Ubuntu with (ONLY) the DVI cable (and your Nvidia card) plugged in (no analogue cable).

[QUOTE=yonkman]Anyway I really just want to get the DVI to work and be able to watch WM9 on xine which, by the way, isn't working now either.[/QUOTE]
You need the codecs for that. Try Automatix, it's VERY easy.

[QUOTE=yonkman]Should I return this card and get a different one???[/QUOTE]
No, I use a GeForce FX 5500 and works great.

First off you should remove the "Option"UseInternalAGPGART" "no""

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-02-04
[QUOTE=Sp@z]wow I got this guide to work once before but I had to reinstall I tried using method 2 and here is what I am getting. I am almost sure it is in the thread somewhere but this thread is HUGE and seraching didn't help either. Thank you

bill@ubuntu:~$ sudo apt-get install linux-image-k7 linux-headers-k7
Reading package lists... Done
Building dependency tree... Done
linux-image-k7 is already the newest version.
linux-headers-k7 is already the newest version.
0 upgraded, 0 newly installed, 0 to remove and 0 not upgraded.
bill@ubuntu:~$ sudo apt-get --purge remove nvidia-glx nvidia-settings
Reading package lists... Done
Building dependency tree... Done
Package nvidia-settings is not installed, so not removed
The following packages will be REMOVED:
  nvidia-glx*
0 upgraded, 0 newly installed, 1 to remove and 0 not upgraded.
Need to get 0B of archives.
After unpacking 10.3MB disk space will be freed.
Do you want to continue [Y/n]? y
dpkg: parse error, in file `/var/lib/dpkg/available' near line 8:
 missing package name
E: Sub-process /usr/bin/dpkg returned an error code (2)
bill@ubuntu:~$[/QUOTE]
It's a problem of APT. Try this.
sudo mv /var/lib/dpkg/available /var/lib/dpkg/available.old1
sudo apt-get update

I hope it fixes the problem

---

### Post by Sp@z on 2006-02-04
[QUOTE=tseliot]It's a problem of APT. Try this.
sudo mv /var/lib/dpkg/available /var/lib/dpkg/available.old1
sudo apt-get update

I hope it fixes the problem[/QUOTE]
Thank you sir! Worked great!

1) this is my primary production workstation and it has taken me two months to get it configured the way I like it
2) the DVI did work at some point in this process, but now does not.

AS for the Xine wm9, I have the codecs installed and it was working fine on my old card/crt set up.  So, I am convinced it has something to do with the new card setup.

I do appreciate the help thanks

### Post by mstlyevil on 2006-02-04
Ts, this is a great guide. I had some issues with my xorg conf file (I made a typo when using Poofy's guide) but I just reconfigured x in recovery mode. The driver installation went flawlessly itself and now works like a dream.

I do have one request. I had to print out the instructions from the thread and it took 10 pages of paper just for the guide. (I had to print out the whole page of the thread so it actually came to 20 pages.) I was wondering if there was a way to post each method in a single page printable format? Maybe some one will offer to host the files and you can provide links on the thread. This will make it a lot easier for those using the guide when they have to exit the gui and finish in text mode. The other option I guess would be to post a link on how to install a text based web browser for use when x is down. Thank you for this guide and your hard work. I appreciate what you have done.

---

### Post by yonkman on 2006-02-04
OK  I went through Method 2 again and this time it worked!!!   This is what I did different

When I used uname -r it would return ........-k7-smp.  I , unfortunately was only using the "k7" part where I needed to add the "uname" part.

When I did it this time I used the whole "k7-smp".

When I ran the install it complained of no kernal interface.  I clicked ok to download and it said none found on NVIDIA site.

However, this time it just proceeded with the install and all seems well.

Here is my new xorg.conf

```
# nvidia-xconfig: X configuration file generated by nvidia-xconfig
# nvidia-xconfig:  version 1.0  (buildmeister@builder3)  Wed Dec 14 16:39:22 PST 2005

# /etc/X11/xorg.conf (xorg X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.
#
# Edit this file with caution, and see the /etc/X11/xorg.conf manual page.
# (Type "man /etc/X11/xorg.conf" at the shell prompt.)
#
# This file is automatically updated on xserver-xorg package upgrades *only*
# if it has not been modified since the last upgrade of the xserver-xorg
# package.
#
# If you have edited this file but would like it to be automatically updated
# again, run the following command:
#   sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg

Section "ServerLayout"
    Identifier     "Default Layout"
    Screen         "Default Screen" 0 0
    InputDevice    "Generic Keyboard"
    InputDevice    "Configured Mouse"
EndSection

Section "Files"

        # paths to defoma fonts
    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/misc"
    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/cyrillic"
    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/100dpi/:unscaled"
    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/75dpi/:unscaled"
    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/Type1"
    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/CID"
    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/100dpi"
    FontPath        "/usr/share/X11/fonts/75dpi"
    FontPath        "/var/lib/defoma/x-ttcidfont-conf.d/dirs/TrueType"
    FontPath        "/var/lib/defoma/x-ttcidfont-conf.d/dirs/CID"
EndSection

Section "Module"
    Load           "bitmap"
    Load           "ddc"
    Load           "extmod"
    Load           "freetype"
    Load           "glx"
    Load           "int10"
    Load           "type1"
    Load           "vbe"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier     "Generic Keyboard"
    Driver         "kbd"
    Option         "CoreKeyboard"
    Option         "XkbRules" "xorg"
    Option         "XkbModel" "pc104"
    Option         "XkbLayout" "us"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier     "Configured Mouse"
    Driver         "mouse"
    Option         "CorePointer"
    Option         "Device" "/dev/input/mice"
    Option         "Protocol" "ImPS/2"
    Option         "ZAxisMapping" "4 5"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
    Identifier     "VA2012wSERIE"
    HorizSync       30.0 - 75.0
    VertRefresh     50.0 - 94.0
    Option         "DPMS"
EndSection

Section "Device"
    Identifier     "NVIDIA Corporation NV34 [GeForce FX 5500]"
    Driver         "nvidia"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
    Identifier     "Default Screen"
    Device         "NVIDIA Corporation NV34 [GeForce FX 5500]"
    Monitor        "VA2012wSERIE"
    DefaultDepth    24
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       1
        Modes      "1680x1050" "1600x1200" "1400x1050" "1280x1024" "1280x960" "1152x864" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       4
        Modes      "1680x1050" "1600x1200" "1400x1050" "1280x1024" "1280x960" "1152x864" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
    EndSubSection

    EndSubSection

        Modes      "1680x1050" "1600x1200" "1400x1050" "1280x1024" "1280x960" "1152x864" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       16
        Modes      "1680x1050" "1600x1200" "1400x1050" "1280x1024" "1280x960" "1152x864" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
    EndSubSection

```

On a side note about the ViewSonic VA2012Wb.   I really like this monitor, but I had to return it to costco FOUR times to get one without bad pixels.  They take them back no questions asked, but be warned if you see this online somewhere

I will now be working on the DVI issue.


Thanks so much for the efforts and contributions to this forum.  I am using linux as my primary business/Development computer now and couldn't be happier that I made the switch.

It wouldn't have been possible without communities such as this.

any thoughts??

When I tried startx after it failed on reboot it gave me the "nvidia version of driver doesn't match that of kernal"  error  (sorry don't have exact copy of it)

hope that helps.

I thought I had it AGHHHHHH[/QUOTE]

You could always reconfigure x in recovery mode. Reboot and start Ubuntu in recovery then when prompted logon with your user name and password. Next enter in this command.

```
sudo dpkg-reconfigure xserver-xorg
```

Just follow the directions and you should be able to get x to restart upon reboot.

---

any thoughts??

When I tried startx after it failed on reboot it gave me the "nvidia version of driver doesn't match that of kernal"  error  (sorry don't have exact copy of it)

hope that helps.

I thought I had it AGHHHHHH[/QUOTE]
Try this:

```
sudo rmmod nvidia
```

and Restart your computer

Then IF you have the same problem try:
```
sudo modprobe nvidia
```

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-02-04
[QUOTE=mstlyevil]I was wondering if there was a way to post each method in a single page printable format? Maybe some one will offer to host the files and you can provide links on the thread. This will make it a lot easier for those using the guide when they have to exit the gui and finish in text mode. The other option I guess would be to post a link on how to install a text based web browser for use when x is down. Thank you for this guide and your hard work. I appreciate what you have done.[/QUOTE]

BTW you can find my entire guide here: [http://doc.gwos.org/index.php/Latest_Nvidia_Breezy]("http://doc.gwos.org/index.php/Latest_Nvidia_Breezy")

And here is a cleaner and shorter version by Artificial Intelligence which is divided in multiple parts: [http://doc.gwos.org/index.php/3D_Graphic_Cards]("http://doc.gwos.org/index.php/3D_Graphic_Cards")

### Post by ivuntu on 2006-02-04
[QUOTE=tseliot]Press CTRL+ALT+F1
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop
cd path_to_the_nvidia_installer
sudo sh name_of_the_nvidia_installer --uninstall
sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf

And set the driver to "vesa" or "nv"

then type:
sudo reboot

After you boot in Ubuntu follow method 2 and try to install the driver again.[/QUOTE]


Good!!  Thank you for that, we are a step further now: the driver installed okay, no error messages this time around. But my screen freezes after a few moments, only the mouse cursor can move, and I cannot switch to text mode anymore. 

BTW: I have an AGP card (Nvidia 6600GT AGP) and I read about the options you can add in this thread, but this did not seem to have any effect.

This is what I added in /etc/X11/xorg.conf :

```
Section "Device"
    Identifier     "Nvidia but using generic vesa"
    Driver         "nvidia"
Option         	"NoAGP" "0"
Option		"RenderAccel" "Off"
Option		"IgnoreDisplayDevices" "DFP,TV"
Option		"NoRenderExtension" " Off"
Option		"Accel" "Off"
Option		"AllowGLXWithComposite" "Off"    	
EndSection
```

Maybe you have a suggestion about this, so the screen won't freeze?

---

### Post by yonkman on 2006-02-04
Damn no luck

What I am trying to figure out is why I was able to go through Method 2, then startx into the kdm with the new nvidia drivers and everything was peachy, but when I rebooted it broke.

What happened when the system restarted that would erase (for lack of better description) the correct nvidia settings?  It is very frustrating.

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-02-04
[QUOTE=ivuntu]Option         	"NoAGP" "0"
Option		"RenderAccel" "Off"
Option		"IgnoreDisplayDevices" "DFP,TV"
Option		"NoRenderExtension" " Off"
Option		"Accel" "Off"
Option		"AllowGLXWithComposite" "Off"    	
EndSection[/CODE]

Maybe you have a suggestion about this, so the screen won't freeze?[/QUOTE]
Ok, you can remove those options if they don't solve the problem.

Follow this guide and use the boot option "noapic" first, otherwise (if for example it causes you problems with the internet connection) try the other options:
[http://www.ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=75281]("http://www.ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=75281")

[QUOTE=yonkman]What happened when the system restarted that would erase (for lack of better description) the correct nvidia settings?  It is very frustrating.[/QUOTE]
I really don't know..

Try this:
sudo cp /boot/initrd.img-`uname -r` ~
sudo mkinitrd -o /boot/initrd.img-`uname -r`

and restart your computer


IF it doesn't solve the problem:
Press CTRL+ALT+F1
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop
sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf

And set the driver to "vesa" or "nv"

then type:
sudo reboot

So that you will have everything up and running again (with "nv" driver).
Then you might want to start a thread on the Unofficial Nvidia Forums

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-02-04
[QUOTE=yonkman]When I tried the image code you suggest it says

"mkinitrd not a command"  Should that happen?[/QUOTE]
Never mind.
If (and ONLY IF) you ALREADY did "sudo cp /boot/initrd.img-`uname -r` ~"
type:
cd ~
sudo cp initrd.img-`uname -r` /boot


and try to start a thread on the Unofficial Nvidia Forums

### Post by ivuntu on 2006-02-05
[QUOTE=tseliot]Ok, you can remove those options if they don't solve the problem.

Follow this guide and use the boot option "noapic" first, otherwise (if for example it causes you problems with the internet connection) try the other options:
[http://www.ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=75281]("http://www.ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=75281")[/QUOTE]

\\:D/ GREAT!!! It's working! I added noapic to the kernel line (and changed the kop-line), and it is still working. Thank you very much for your time and patience, tseliot, I really appreciate it!! It's been very educative! 

One suggestion for the above method: when you save /boot/grub/menu.lst and reboot the first time, you have to edit the kernel line again, otherwise the screen will freeze again.




For those who might be interested, here's what I learned/concluded:

 *  I followed method 2 to install the latest drivers (version 1.0.8178 im my case), but before that I had tried other solutions, so I did not start with a clean slate. Probably some previous installation messed things up, so I got the different version error (X didn't start because the NVIDIA kernel module was version 1.0.7667, but the X module was version 1.0.8178 ).

 *  the last time I followed method 2 I first uninstalled any previous installations like this: 

> Press CTRL+ALT+F1
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop
cd path_to_the_nvidia_installer
sudo sh name_of_the_nvidia_installer --uninstall
sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf

And set the driver to "vesa" or "nv"

then type:
sudo reboot

After you boot in Ubuntu follow method 2 and try to install the driver again.

 * then, the driver did work, but the screen kept freezing, except for the mouse cursor. 

 * to fix this, I followed the instructions you see in the quote on top of this reply. Adding noapic to the kernel command line worked, and I checked the CPU resource, which are much lower then before.

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-02-05
[QUOTE=ivuntu]One suggestion for the above method: when you save /boot/grub/menu.lst and reboot the first time, you have to edit the kernel line again, otherwise the screen will freeze again.[/QUOTE]
After you edit /boot/grub/menu.lst you have to type "sudo update-grub" so that the option is passed to all the kernel lines

### Post by tseliot on 2006-02-05
[QUOTE=Joe]Any idea why the driver only seems to work using vga and not dvi?  Let me add that I'm using a Princeton vl1716 monitor.  Here's my xorg.conf:[/QUOTE]

Add the option in red to your xorg.conf:

```
Section "Device"
	Identifier	"NVIDIA Corporation NV34 [GeForce FX 5200]"
	Driver		"nvidia"
	BusID		"PCI:1:0:0"
Option "RenderAccel" "On"
#Option "IgnoreDisplayDevices" "DFP,TV"
 Option "NoRenderExtension" "On"
 Option "Accel" "On"
 Option "AllowGLXWithComposite" "Off"
[COLOR="Red"]Option "ConnectedMonitor" "DFP-0"[/COLOR]
EndSection
```

Then Log out, turn off your monitor. 
Plug in the DVI cable and unplug VGA one. 
Then Turn on your monitor and press CTRL+ALT+BACKSPACE.

Tell me if it works

### Post by tseliot on 2006-02-05
[QUOTE=FraM]Before this, I have a problem :cry: 
X doesn't start anymore!
At boot, the X log says that API of kernel module version (7667) mismatch driver version (8179). 

What could I do?

I followed carefully method 2. I successfully restarted GDM after installation and everything was fine (apart from skype). Then I reboot my machine and it happened what I wrote.[/QUOTE]
Do this:
Press CTRL+ALT+F1
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop
cd path_to_the_nvidia_installer
sudo sh name_of_the_nvidia_installer --uninstall
sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf

And set the driver to "vesa" or "nv"

sudo reboot

The xserver won't fail.

Then reinstall the drivers using method 1.

### Post by tseliot on 2006-02-05
[QUOTE=FraM]I've followed your procedure and I got back my X server working.
You wrote to install nvidia driver by method 1: does this mean I cannot use Nvidia 81.78 driver?

Thanks for your help! :-D[/QUOTE]
Yours is a common problem. I think you'd better use method 1 for now until I find a way to fix the problem (permanently).

However if you want to try this:
sudo apt-get --purge remove nvidia-glx linux-restricted-modules-`uname -r`

Set the driver to "nvidia" in your xorg.conf and restart you computer.

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-02-06
UPDATE: For all the users who have the following problem:

At boot, the X server complains about the "API of kernel module version (7667) mismatch driver version (8178)"

Try this:
sudo apt-get --purge remove nvidia-glx nvidia-settings [COLOR="Red"]nvidia-kernel-common[/COLOR]

I've fixed the guide. It's very important to remove nvidia-kernel-common.

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-02-06
[QUOTE=Norwal]Hello tseliot,

I have been struggling with this for two days now. Initially i was have problems at step 7 & 8 of method 2.  I was getting a message that said "no such file or directory". It turns of that the instruction I printed out missed the "nvidia-kernel-common" part of the command. 

I wipe out my HD and started over figuring a clean start would be best.  I am using the complete 2.6.12-10-k7, have a N6600 vidio card with dual monitor(not being used), and I am attempting to use the ia32 8178 drivers from nvidia.

Every think seem to go fine. But when  I attempted to Restart or Shut down the computer hangs here:

Skipping /usr/X11R6/lib/modules/libfb.a:fbmmx.o no symbols found.
Warning: font renderer for "pcf" already registered at priority 0
Warning: ''   '' ''  pcf.Z '' ''
Warning:  " " pct.gz
Warning: " " snf
Warning: " " snf.Z "
etc....

If I type startx I up and running again.

I'm not sure to understand. Do you get the error at boot?
If so, I need you to reproduce the error.

When GDM (or kdm) crashes at start-up Press CTRL+ALT+F1
Then type

Then:
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```

```
startx -- -verbose 5 -logverbose 5
```

You will get the same error.

sudo cp /var/log/Xorg.0.log /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log
(Of course you have to replace "your_username" with your username)

and post the content of that file (which you can find under /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log )


BTW if you want to access the GNOME or KDE again you can use this quick fix:
```
sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf
```
Set the driver back to "nv" instead of "nvidia"
CTRL+O to save
CTRL+X to exit

```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```

Then
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm start (if you use GDM)
```

### Post by tseliot on 2006-02-07
[QUOTE=Joe]Didn't work.  It doesn't boot into X and I get the following error:

Fatal: Module nvidia not found.
(EE) Nvidia (0): Aborting
(EE): Screens found, but none have usable configuration.[/QUOTE]
:-k  remove the option I suggested before.

Boot ONLY with your DVI cable plugged in and with the nvidia driver.
The xserver will give you an error.
Press CTRL+ALT+F1
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM, or "kdm stop" if you use KDM)
You will be able to type the following commands in the command line:
sudo dpkg-reconfigure xserver-xorg

It will ask you several questions: select the Nvidia driver and you monitor (if you use the "advanced" function you can set the Horizontal and Vertical refresh rate).
If you don't know the answer to a question just press ENTER (so as to use the default answer).
If you want to select a resolution or anything else by putting a cross in a checkbox you have to select the desired checkbox and press the SPACEBAR. After that you can press ENTER to go to the next question.

When you have finished, type:
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm start (if you use GDM, or "kdm start" if you use KDM)

I hope it works

---

Type these commands (just COPY and PASTE the commands as I've written):
```
sudo apt-get install linux-restricted-modules-`uname -r`-nvidia-legacy nvidia-glx-legacy
```

Then set the driver to nvidia in your xorg.conf. Log out and press CTRL+ALT+Backspace.

### Post by tseliot on 2006-02-08
[QUOTE=at0000]thx for trying to help me, but my english is not great so you can dont understund what I sed:) I try compiled drivers (from nvidia www) AND legacy drivers from apt (yes, nvidia-glx-legacy ...etc) AND this dont work. Maybe any other sugestions?[/QUOTE]
I should have payed more attention to your post.

1) Try Point 4 in the PROBLEMS SECTION.

2) Moreover I have a question for you:
What's the model of your graphic card?

3) the Nvidia installer might not work in Dapper. Only method 1 and method 3 (don't use method 3, it's almost useless ;) ) are reported to work in Dapper. As a matter of fact this guide is for Breezy.

Therefore I suggest you to uninstall the driver which you installed with method 2 (in order to avoid conflicts between the different versions) do this:
cd path_to_the_nvidia_installer (i.e. the folder in which you put the Nvidia installer)
sudo sh name_of_your_nvidia_installer --uninstall

### Post by tseliot on 2006-02-08
[QUOTE=at0000]NVIDIA Corporation NV5M64 [RIVA TNT2 Model 64/Model 64 
Pro



You know what? There WAS conflict! when I have module from apt I dont uninstall that from nvidia. How can I forget? Big thx for show it :D Now I do that. And it's anymore that error from startx. Now it's new one ;) It looks that:
(EE) NVIDIA(0): Failed to initialize the NVIDIA kernel module!
(EE) NVIDIA(0):  *** Aborting ***
(II) UnloadModule: "nvidia"
(EE) Screen(s) found, but none have a usable configuration.

What I saw it problem can be with difrent version of glx driver and kernel (first - 7174, 2. - 7169? ). I download everything from apt now. If I have right - what to change?[/QUOTE]
Try this:
set the driver to "nv"
Then log out and press CTRL+ALT+Backspace and log in.

```
sudo apt-get --purge remove linux-restricted-modules-`uname -r`-nvidia-legacy
sudo apt-get --purge remove nvidia-glx-legacy nvidia-settings nvidia-kernel-common
```

```
Then sudo apt-get install linux-restricted-modules-`uname -r` nvidia-glx nvidia-settings
```
(you don't need to install nvidia-kernel-common)

Then set the driver to "nvidia" in your xorg.conf log out, press CTRL+ALT+Backspace and log in

[COLOR="Red"]IF it doesn't work yet[/COLOR], I need you to reproduce the error.
Therefore set the driver to "nvidia" in your xorg.conf
When GDM (or kdm) crashes at start-up Press CTRL+ALT+F1
Then type

Then:
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```

```
startx -- -verbose 5 -logverbose 5
```

You will get the same error.

sudo cp /var/log/Xorg.0.log /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log
(Of course you have to replace "your_username" with your username)

and post the content of that file (which you can find under /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log )

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-02-09
[QUOTE=at0000]yes, I need it. apt thinks that. and want to install it, even if i don't adding it ;)
2nd thing - there's no such package like linux-restricted-modules-XXX-nvidia-legacy (you want me to install/remove it). Theres is only linux-restricted-modules-XXX (where XXX is `uname -r` ofcorse).

anyway, I remove all these packages, install them once again (with nvidia-kernel-common - like I seid it before, apt want it).. no diffrence. 

And the last part of error:[/QUOTE]
There's a problem: if you want to use nvidia-glx-[COLOR="Red"]legacy[/COLOR] you NEED linux-restricted-modules-`uname -r`-nvidia-[COLOR="Red"]legacy[/COLOR]

Everything must be "legacy" otherwise you will get a module mismatch.

Perhaps the package linux-restricted-modules-`uname -r`-nvidia-[COLOR="Red"]legacy[/COLOR] is not available in Dapper repositories at the moment. I really don't know as, at the moment, my guide doesn't cover Dapper.

[QUOTE=BanskuZ]Aagrh. Fonts are now ugly :mad: .

Old desktop:
[[IMG]http://img206.imageshack.us/img206/6796/munlosttyopoyta0zc.th.jpg[/IMG]](http://img206.imageshack.us/my.php?image=munlosttyopoyta0zc.jpg)

When I installed drivers:
[[IMG]http://img440.imageshack.us/img440/1427/aagrhfonts3xk.th.png[/IMG]](http://img440.imageshack.us/my.php?image=aagrhfonts3xk.png)

Xorg.conf: [http://paste.servut.us/index.php?id=107668468343eb6e2a7f965](http://paste.servut.us/index.php?id=107668468343eb6e2a7f965)[/QUOTE]
That never happened to me and nobody had reported such a problem (at least not in this guide).

Try this:
cd /etc/X11
ls xorg*
You will see a list of files
you should have a file named "xorg.conf.backup-nvidia-glx" or "xorg.conf.backup"

then type:
sudo cp name_of_that_file xorg.conf

Then log out and press CTRL+ALT+Backspace

Then let me know if it solves the problem (so as to see if everything works fine with your previous settings)

### Post by tseliot on 2006-02-09
[QUOTE=barrosz]hi, im a real noob, i want to thank you for this guide, i updated to latest drivers using the 2nd method. i have a few questions though, when i $ cat /proc/driver/nvidia/agp/status , i get:
Status:          Enabled
Driver:          AGPGART
AGP Rate:        4x
Fast Writes:     Disabled
SBA:             Disabled

now my questions are: why is the driver agpgart instead of nvidia? and how can i enable the FW and SBA settings? must be some config file but i dont know much about linux, thanks in advance.[/QUOTE]
There's nothing wrong with AGPGART. Remember: if it works don't try to fix it ;) .

Type this to know if your card supports Fast writes and SBA:
cat /proc/driver/nvidia/agp/host-bridge

### Post by tseliot on 2006-02-11
[QUOTE=Temple]Um, I tried doing method one and it said it couldn't find my module which was 2.6.2 something. I'm a total linux noob, but I'm trying to learn however I use an nvidia card, and apt-get nvidia-glx etcetc and It said my module found or something similar (sorry for my not writing down the text) Any help?[/QUOTE]
If so, I need you to reproduce the error.

When GDM (or kdm) crashes at start-up Press CTRL+ALT+F1
Then type

Then:
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```

```
startx -- -verbose 5 -logverbose 5
```

You will get the same error.

sudo cp /var/log/Xorg.0.log /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log
(Of course you have to replace "your_username" with your username)

and post the content of that file (which you can find under /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log )


BTW if you want to access the GNOME or KDE again you can use this quick fix:
```
sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf
```
Set the driver back to "nv" instead of "nvidia"
CTRL+O to save
CTRL+X to exit

```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```

Then
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm start (if you use GDM)
```

### Post by mips on 2006-02-11
Great guide tseliot !

I used Method 2 to install the x86 8178 driver.

I had one problem though, when trying to run the installer in step 10 I got the message to find a precompiled kernel from the nVidia website to which I said OK but nothing happened and the installer just sat there doing nothing. I quit via ctrl-c and redid that step. This time it came back saying no precompiled kernel found and would I like to compile one now and after that everything was plain sailing.

I also noticed that under Apllications->System 2 new entries were created (No Icon)NVIDEA Settings & (Icon)NVIDEA X Server Settings.

Scrolling through windows in Konquerer is like lightning now !!!

Also noticed my fonts have changed ? Not complaining as they look much better now, no longer thick&fat as before !!!

Maybe you should mention in the Howto that people using Kubuntu should use **kdesu kate** instead of sudo kate.

### Post by tappad on 2006-02-11
Hi!

I'm having some problems with NVIDIA drivers.
Lots of programs won't run eg. Mplayer, xmms + some others (Segmentation fault, something like that). I have discovered that it all works fine if i just reinstall nvidia package (with synaptic). However, as soon as my computer is rebooted the problem reappers, so i have to do this on every boot and it's getting frustrating.. Any ideas?

Also, if i enable glx (nvidia-glx-config enable).. X wont start at the next reboot.. So then i have to reload Xorg.conf, and everything will be ok..

Here is what happens:
Everything boots fine, all the way till its time to start X (before login).
The system seems too freeze up and the upper half of the screen is filled with black (looks like its still in "console mode", or whatever) and the bottom half is filled with white colour. For about 5 seconds i can se the _ cursor (not the mousepointer but the inputcursor). Nothing really happens when i press any keys except, some flicker in the white regions when ALT + f1-f4 (maybe all the buttons, havent tried them all).
The first 2 times this happened i got an error message (blue screen, red box in the center) that X crashed and if i wanted to se errorlogs. However, i cant see them.. becouse i am promted  to enter my username/password. (Error message freezes in the background, it's in console mode.)

The first time it happened was when i pressed CTRL+ ALT + Backspace to restart X, becouse the system got slower, and also VLC was hung up (Witch it does some times, usually no big prob.)

I've followed your guide, but in the end it just wont work.
I also dont know where this errorlog is located. Probably there is some help to be found there..

Im using breezy and have a fx5200 128mb Graphics card.

Thanks for any replys.

1) Did you try to installer the drivers with method 2 before or after method 1? Don't do that, I just want to know if there might be a conflict of some sort between the drivers.

2) post the content of your /etc/X11/xorg.conf

3) I need you to reproduce the error.

Set the nvidia driver in your /etc/X11/xorg.conf (or if it's already set leave it as it is.
When GDM (or kdm) crashes at startup Press CTRL+ALT+F1
Then type

Then:
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```

```
startx -- -verbose 5 -logverbose 5
```

You will get the same error.

sudo cp /var/log/Xorg.0.log /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log
(Of course you have to replace "your_username" with your username)

and post the content of that file (which you can find under /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log )


BTW if you want to access the GNOME or KDE again you can use this quick fix:
```
sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf
```
Set the driver back to "nv" instead of "nvidia"
CTRL+O to save
CTRL+X to exit

```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```

Then
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm start (if you use GDM)
```

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-02-11
[QUOTE=tappad]> 1) Did you try to installer the drivers with method 2 before or after method 1? Don't do that, I just want to know if there might be a conflict of some sort between the drivers.[QUOTE]
I think ive only done method 1, But, its possible ive messed up big time :(

I cant do anything after this happns. When i press CTRL+ALT+F1(F2,F3..)
All i get is some flicker in the white area.. ctrl, alt + del doesnt work either.

But here is the xorg.conf (log in separate post, too long post...)

Thanks for the help!
Sorry if I'm a bit slow today (I haven't slept enough and it's night in Italy) but I don't understand if you are able to type anything after the error of the xserver. In other words did you set the driver to "nv" instead of nvidia in your xorg.conf (see the example below)?

sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf
Set the driver back to "nv" instead of "nvidia"
CTRL+O to save
CTRL+X to exit
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm start (if you use GDM)

Anyhow if I misunderstood what you wrote and you can't even get to the command line you have to boot in Recovery mode (the xserver won't start and you will be able to use the command line and set the driver to "nv").
If you don't know how to boot in Recovery mode:
Turn on your computer and keep pressing "ESC" until you get to the GRUB menu. Select Recovery mode.

Please tell me if I misunderstood the whole thing.

I'll be back tomorrow.

Good night

---

### Post by tappad on 2006-02-11
Sorry if I'm a bit slow today (I haven't slept enough and it's night in Italy) but I don't understand if you are able to type anything after the error of the xserver. In other words did you set the driver to "nv" instead of nvidia in your xorg.conf (see the example below)?

sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf
Set the driver back to "nv" instead of "nvidia"
CTRL+O to save
CTRL+X to exit
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm start (if you use GDM)

Anyhow if I misunderstood what you wrote and you can't even get to the command line you have to boot in Recovery mode (the xserver won't start and you will be able to use the command line and set the driver to "nv").
If you don't know how to boot in Recovery mode:
Turn on your computer and keep pressing "ESC" until you get to the GRUB menu. Select Recovery mode.

Please tell me if I misunderstood the whole thing.

ALSA lib pcm_dmix.c:802: 9snd_pcm_dmix_open unable to open slave       [[COLOR="Red"]fail[/COLOR]]

The computer freezes at this point and wont accept any input.

### Post by jimbaker on 2006-02-13
Re: Riva TNT2 64
Just thought I'd mention that I performed Method #1 above and all seemed to go well until I restarted X, or tried to...
I can't recall the errors exactly, but it had something to do with no being able to use any of the resolutions or something like that.
Well, I was at a friends house trying to get him going on his new Ubuntu, and this happened.
I came home, hooked the box back up to my keyboard, mouse, and monitor, and voila, X came up just fine (Gnome).
Hmmm...
Well, obviously it must have something to do with the fact that I'm using an LCD monitor, relatively new, and he had an old clunker crt.
So searching the web yielded the following command for reconfiguring X:
sudo dpkg-reconfigure xserver.xorg
I ran this (while still connected to my LCD) and answered as generically (thinking clunker crt and *simple* the whole time) as possible.
X came up again, but I still don't know if it will on his system.
Next step, go back to his place and try the command above while connected to his monitor.
Or, get the info for his monitor and plug it in here...
I'll probably go with the first one.
Thanks for all the help!
I'm learning, and that's the fun part, right?
\\:D/

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-02-13
[QUOTE=k0fcc]Howdy, 

I've been getting errors using the nvidia driver for 1920x1200 on my 6800 ultra.
The nv driver works great but the nvidia driver from apt does not.

 
and my xorg.conf is (in nv state, comments show you what I remove for nvidia)
 

I should point out this diff:


So it appears that the nvidia driver's clock range is off.

Any help would be most appreciated.  I seem to recall hearing about a bug in the driver but don't know for certain.[/QUOTE]

1) Add the option in red to your xorg.conf and set the driver to nvidia:
```
Section "Device"
Identifier "NVIDIA Corporation NV40 [GeForce 6800 Ultra/GeForce 6800 GT]"
Driver "nvidia"
BusID "PCI:1:0:0"
VideoRam 256
#next 2 for composite rendering with official nvidia driver only
#Option "RenderAccel" "true"
#Option "AllowGLXWithComposite" "true" 
[COLOR="Red"]Option "IgnoreEDID" "True"[/COLOR]
EndSection
```

Save the file and log out and press CTRL+ALT+Backspace.

2) IF that didn't work then I need you to reproduce the error.

Set the nvidia driver in your /etc/X11/xorg.conf (or if it's already set leave it as it is.
When GDM (or kdm) crashes at startup Press CTRL+ALT+F1
Then type

Then:
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```

```
startx -- -verbose 5 -logverbose 5
```

You will get the same error.

sudo cp /var/log/Xorg.0.log /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log
(Of course you have to replace "your_username" with your username)

and post the content of that file (which you can find under /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log )


BTW if you want to access the GNOME or KDE again you can use this quick fix:
```
sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf
```
Set the driver back to "nv" instead of "nvidia"
CTRL+O to save
CTRL+X to exit

```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```

Then
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm start (if you use GDM)
```

---

and part 2

>

### Post by tendo on 2006-02-13
[QUOTE=handy]I've just set my 6800GT up using the most simple & quick method below: +++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++ Install Nvidia driver From Ubuntu Document Storage Facility Written by: Artificial Intelligence [edit] HOWTO: Install Nvidia driver in Ubuntu - The Default Open the terminal and write: sudo apt-get install nvidia-glx sudo apt-get install nvidia-settings sudo nvidia-glx-config enable smeg In the left window of the menu editor choose System Tools, then click the New Entry button. Entry Editor Name: Nvidia Settings Comment: <What ever you like> Command: nvidia-settings Icon: <Pick a icon for it or just leave it> Press the OK button. Reboot +++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++ The only bit that didn't work is the "nVidia Settings" menu entry at the end, I'll sort that out later. Glxgears: 59460 frames in 5.0 seconds = 11891.943 FPS 65675 frames in 5.0 seconds = 13134.952 FPS 65689 frames in 5.0 seconds = 13137.785 FPS 65678 frames in 5.0 seconds = 13135.452 FPS 65678 frames in 5.0 seconds = 13135.535 FPS 65682 frames in 5.0 seconds = 13136.269 FPS I was getting about 20 FPS before the driver install!!! Very Happy :D handy[/QUOTE] I have a 6800GT and I'm using kubuntu. I don't understand what " In the left window of the menu editor choose System Tools, then click the New Entry button." means though. Could someone tell me how to do this part in kubuntu please. I'm hoping its ok to ask kubuntu questions in this forum I don't really know. :???:

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-02-14
[QUOTE=tendo]I have a 6800GT and I'm using kubuntu. I don't understand what " In the left window of the menu editor choose System Tools, then click the New Entry button." means though. Could someone tell me how to do this part in kubuntu please. I'm hoping its ok to ask kubuntu questions in this forum I don't really know. :???:[/QUOTE]
Yes, it's ok to ask questions about Kubuntu here ;) .
Click on the applications menu (or the K menu or whatever you have). Get your mouse cursor on the "System tools" and right click and select "Edit menu".
The menu editor will show up.
Click on "File" > "New item" and follow Artificial Intelligence's instructions. Only 1 thing: remember to save the menu entry you have added by clicking on "File" > "Save"

---

### Post by tseliot on 2006-02-14
[QUOTE=k0fcc]and part 2[/QUOTE]
1) How many monitors do you have plugged in?
2) Did you try Heimo's guide?
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=83973&highlight=resolution]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=83973&highlight=resolution")
3) OR (Even better in this case) did you try this guide? [HOWTO: Get Apple Cinema 23" Display working using Nvidia drivers]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=85109&highlight=Apple+cinema")

### Post by joey on 2006-02-14
...and the new non-apple (Go figure) modeline worked!!!!  I was using the ones directly from apple. 

So here is the working config (albeit with some minor WW's still).

```

# /etc/X11/xorg.conf (xorg X Window System server configuration file)
#
# This file was generated by dexconf, the Debian X Configuration tool, using
# values from the debconf database.
#
# Edit this file with caution, and see the /etc/X11/xorg.conf manual page.
# (Type "man /etc/X11/xorg.conf" at the shell prompt.)
#
# This file is automatically updated on xserver-xorg package upgrades *only*
# if it has not been modified since the last upgrade of the xserver-xorg
# package.
#
# If you have edited this file but would like it to be automatically updated
# again, run the following command:
#   sudo dpkg-reconfigure -phigh xserver-xorg

Section "Files"
    FontPath    "/usr/share/X11/fonts/misc"
    FontPath    "/usr/share/X11/fonts/cyrillic"
    FontPath    "/usr/share/X11/fonts/100dpi/:unscaled"
    FontPath    "/usr/share/X11/fonts/75dpi/:unscaled"
    FontPath    "/usr/share/X11/fonts/Type1"
    FontPath    "/usr/share/X11/fonts/CID"
    FontPath    "/usr/share/X11/fonts/100dpi"
    FontPath    "/usr/share/X11/fonts/75dpi"
     # paths to defoma fonts
    FontPath "/var/lib/defoma/x-ttcidfont-conf.d/dirs/TrueType"
    FontPath "/var/lib/defoma/x-ttcidfont-conf.d/dirs/CID"
EndSection

Section "Module"
    #comment out GLcore & dir for nvidia drivers
    #Load    "GLcore"
    #Load    "dri"
    Load    "i2c"
    Load    "bitmap"
    Load    "ddc"
    Load    "extmod"
    Load    "freetype"
    Load    "glx"
    Load    "int10"
    Load    "type1"
    Load    "vbe"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier    "Generic Keyboard"
    Driver        "kbd"
    Option        "CoreKeyboard"
    Option        "XkbRules"    "xorg"
    Option        "XkbModel"    "pc104"
    Option        "XkbLayout"    "us"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier    "Configured Mouse"
    Driver        "mouse"
    Option        "CorePointer"
    Option        "Device"        "/dev/input/mice"
    Option        "Protocol"        "ExplorerPS/2"
    Option        "Emulate3Buttons"    "false"
    Option        "ZAxisMapping"        "4 5"
    Option        "Buttons"        "5"
EndSection

Section "Device"
    Identifier    "NVIDIA Corporation NV40 [GeForce 6800 Ultra/GeForce 6800 GT]"
    #Driver        "nv"
    Driver        "nvidia"
    BusID        "PCI:1:0:0"
    VideoRam    256
    #next 2 for composite rendering with official nvidia driver only
        Option          "RenderAccel"           "true"
        Option          "AllowGLXWithComposite" "true" 
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
    Identifier    "Apple Cinema"
    Option        "DPMS"
    #others from the web
    #HorizSync    30.0-130.0
    #VertRefresh    60.0-90.0
    #autogenerated
    HorizSync    28-96
    VertRefresh    43-60
    #
    # 1920x1200 @ 60Hz - From Apple
    # Modeline "1920x1200" 193.156 1920 2048 2256 2592 1200 1201 1203 1242 +hsync +vsync
    # 1920X1200 - from Ubuntu Forums
     Modeline "1920x1200" 155.0 1920 1984 2016 2144 1200 1203 1206 1212 -HSync +Vsync
EndSection

Section "Screen"
    Identifier    "Default Screen"
    Device        "NVIDIA Corporation NV40 [GeForce 6800 Ultra/GeForce 6800 GT]"
    Monitor        "Apple Cinema"
    DefaultDepth    24
    SubSection "Display"
        Depth        1
         Modes "1920x1200" "1680x1050" "1280x960" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection "Display"
        Depth        4
         Modes "1920x1200" "1680x1050" "1280x960" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection "Display"
        Depth        8
         Modes "1920x1200" "1680x1050" "1280x960" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection "Display"
        Depth        15
         Modes "1920x1200" "1680x1050" "1280x960" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection "Display"
        Depth        16
         Modes "1920x1200" "1680x1050" "1280x960" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
    SubSection "Display"
        Depth        24
         Modes "1920x1200" "1680x1050" "1280x960" "1024x768" "800x600" "640x480"
    EndSubSection
EndSection

Section "ServerLayout"
    Identifier    "Default Layout"
    Screen        "Default Screen"
    InputDevice    "Generic Keyboard"
    InputDevice    "Configured Mouse"
EndSection

Section "DRI"
    Mode    0666
EndSection

```

---

### Post by joey on 2006-02-14
Howdy TS...

### Post by tseliot on 2006-02-16
[QUOTE=acorrigan]I followed the instructions exactly, but I got an error message that the nvidia module is already loaded.  To get back into KDE I rebooted, but now my computer stopped booting :([/QUOTE]
I need you to reproduce the error.

Set the nvidia driver in your /etc/X11/xorg.conf (or if it's already set leave it as it is.
When GDM (or kdm) crashes at startup Press CTRL+ALT+F1
Then type

Then:
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```
OR
```
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm stop (if you use KDM)
```

```
startx -- -verbose 5 -logverbose 5
```

You will get the same error.

sudo cp /var/log/Xorg.0.log /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log
(Of course you have to replace "your_username" with your username)

and post the content of that file (which you can find under /home/your_username/Xorg.0.log )


BTW if you want to access the GNOME or KDE again you can use this quick fix:
```
sudo nano /etc/X11/xorg.conf
```
Set the driver back to "nv" instead of "nvidia"
CTRL+O to save
CTRL+X to exit

```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm stop (if you use GDM)
```
OR
```
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm stop (if you use KDM)
```

Then
```
sudo /etc/init.d/gdm start (if you use GDM)
```
OR
```
sudo /etc/init.d/kdm start (if you use KDM)
```

### Post by PsTJsT on 2006-02-17
Okay, my experience comes with good news and bad news...

The good news is that I didn't encounter any problems while following Method 2 and I've installed the latest nVidia drivers for my card.

The bad news is that as soon as I completed the process, I lost my wireless device (ath0).  It doesn't show up with either "lspci" or "iwconfig -a".  Additionally, "ifup ath0" states the device isn't found/valid.  The device (a D-Link DWL-G520 w/ an Atheros chipset) has been working fine with the standard madwifi drivers ever since I installed Breezy several weeks ago, and I'm pretty sure it's not a hardware issue because it's identified and works when I boot with a Breezy live cd.

An unrelated thread suggested the fix for a similar problem experienced by someone else was "apt-get install linux-restricted-modules-$(uname -r)" but that doesn't seem to be an option for me since I can't get online (with that computer) and, even if I could, I'm not sure how this would impact my new nVidia drivers.

Any ideas?

P.S.  I encountered a prompt not mentioned in the How-To.  In "step 10," after the installer asked whether I wanted to compile a module for my kernel, I was prompted to allow the installer to modify my xorg.conf file so that the drivers could be started automatically and, at the same time, to allow the installer to create a backup of my xorg.conf file.  This was before "step 11" of Method 2, so answering yes to that prompt would result in the backup suggested in step 11 being nothing more than a backup of the modified xorg.conf file.  Correct?
